# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section uses **Mermaid block diagrams** to visualize PPC architecture and auction flow — directly useful for KTU diagram-based questions (often asked for 3–5 marks).

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1: PPC Campaign Account Hierarchy

This diagram shows the structural architecture of a Google Ads account — a frequently tested concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ACCOUNT LEVEL] --> B0[CAMPAIGN LEVEL]
    B0 --> C0[AD GROUP 1]
    B0 --> C1[AD GROUP 2]
    B0 --> C2[AD GROUP 3]
    C0 --> D0[KEYWORD A]
    C0 --> D1[KEYWORD B]
    C0 --> E0[TEXT AD 1]
    C0 --> E1[TEXT AD 2]
    C0 --> F0[LANDING PAGE URL]
    C1 --> D2[KEYWORD C]
    C1 --> E2[TEXT AD 3]
    C2 --> D3[KEYWORD D]
    C2 --> E3[SHOPPING AD 1]

    style A0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B0 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

**Explanation of Layers:**
- **Account Level:** The master login, billing, and settings.
- **Campaign Level:** Defines the budget, geographic targeting, network (Search/Display), and bidding strategy.
- **Ad Group Level:** Contains a tightly themed set of keywords and their associated ads.
- **Keyword/Ad Level:** The actual match types and creative variants.
- **Landing Page:** The destination URL the user is sent to after the click.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule:** Each Ad Group should have a **tight keyword-to-ad relevance**. Mixing unrelated keywords (e.g., "running shoes" and "kitchen knives") in one ad group hurts Quality Score and raises CPC.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Real-Time Ad Auction Flow

This shows the millisecond-level sequence of events when a user searches.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U0[USER TYPES SEARCH QUERY] --> Q0[QUERY SENT TO AD PLATFORM]
    Q0 --> A0[AUCTION TRIGGERED FOR MATCHING KEYWORDS]
    A0 --> R0[AD RANK CALCULATION]
    R0 --> R1[Step 1 Check Max CPC Bid]
    R0 --> R2[Step 2 Check Quality Score]
    R0 --> R3[Step 3 Check Ad Extensions Impact]
    R1 --> S0[ADS RANKED IN DESCENDING ORDER]
    R2 --> S0
    R3 --> S0
    S0 --> D0[TOP SLOTS DISPLAYED ON SERP]
    D0 --> C0[USER CLICKS AN AD]
    C0 --> B0[ADVERTISER CHARGED EFFECTIVE CPC]
    B0 --> T0[CONVERSION TRACKING PIXEL FIRES]
    T0 --> O0[DATA LOGGED TO AD ACCOUNT DASHBOARD]

    style U0 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style A0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style S0 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style O0 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Ad Rank Formula Matters for the Exam:** A question like "An advertiser bids ₹50 with a Quality Score of 4, while a competitor bids ₹80 with a Quality Score of 9. Who wins?" tests your understanding of the multiplier formula. Calculate both Ad Ranks:
> Advertiser A: $50 \times 4 = 200$
> Advertiser B: $80 \times 9 = 720$
> → Advertiser B wins despite higher relative bid efficiency.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: PPC Conversion Funnel (Block Architecture)

This block-level architecture shows user progression from impression to revenue, useful for understanding attribution and bottlenecks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[STAGE 1 IMPRESSIONS] --> L1[STAGE 2 CLICKS]
    L1 --> L2[STAGE 3 LANDING PAGE VISIT]
    L2 --> L3[STAGE 4 CONVERSION ACTION]
    L3 --> L4[STAGE 5 REVENUE]

    L0 -. CTR 3 percent .-> L1
    L1 -. CVR 4 percent .-> L3
    L3 -. AOV 1500 rupees .-> L4

    style L0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style L2 fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style L3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style L4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Funnel Matters in Optimization:** The "weakest link" in the funnel determines campaign success. A great ad with a 10% CTR fails if the landing page has a 0.1% CVR. Hence, every stage is a separate optimization lever (ad copy, landing page UX, checkout flow).

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section follows the **KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**: Part A (3 marks each) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice, split into 7+7 sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set A]`
**Q: Define Pay-Per-Click (PPC) advertising. List any four popular PPC platforms.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Pay-Per-Click (PPC) is a digital advertising model in which advertisers pay a fee to the ad platform each time a user clicks on their advertisement. It is a form of paid search marketing that enables advertisers to bid on keywords relevant to their target audience, ensuring ads are shown to users with active commercial intent.

**Four PPC Platforms (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
1. Google Ads (formerly Google AdWords)
2. Microsoft Advertising (Bing Ads)
3. Meta Ads Manager (Facebook + Instagram)
4. LinkedIn Ads / Twitter Ads / Amazon Ads (any one)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**Q: Differentiate between CPC and CPM bidding models with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | CPC (Cost Per Click) | CPM (Cost Per Mille) |
|---|---|---|
| **Charging Criterion** | Advertiser pays per click | Advertiser pays per 1,000 impressions |
| **Risk on** | Low traffic, no clicks | Low engagement, many views no clicks |
| **Best For** | Direct response / lead gen | Brand awareness / reach campaigns |
| **Example** | A law firm bidding "divorce lawyer near me" at ₹100 CPC | A new cola brand paying ₹500 CPM for a video ad view |

**Formula Distinction (½ mark):**
$$\text{CPC} = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Clicks}}, \quad \text{CPM} = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 1000$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Main Paper]`

**Q: "Pay-Per-Click is the most accountable form of digital advertising." Critically analyse this statement. As part of your answer:**
**(a) Explain the working mechanism of Google Ads auction, including the Ad Rank formula. (7 marks)**
**(b) The following data is provided for a 30-day PPC campaign of an online bookstore. Calculate CTR, CPC, CVR, CPA, and ROAS, and comment on profitability. (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Impressions = 60,000
- Clicks = 2,400
- Conversions = 120
- Total Ad Spend = ₹36,000
- Total Revenue from Ads = ₹1,20,000

---

#### Part A(a) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Introduction to Google Ads Auction (2 marks)**
The Google Ads auction is a real-time, second-price auction triggered every time a user performs a search. Unlike traditional auctions, the highest bidder does not always win. Google evaluates ads using the **Ad Rank formula** to ensure that users see ads most relevant to their query.

**Step 2: Ad Rank Formula (3 marks)**
$$\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Max CPC Bid} \times \text{Quality Score} \times \text{Expected Impact of Ad Extensions}$$

- **Max CPC Bid:** The maximum amount the advertiser is willing to pay per click.
- **Quality Score (1–10):** A diagnostic tool that estimates the quality of ads, keywords, and landing pages. It depends on:
  - Expected CTR
  - Ad relevance
  - Landing page experience
- **Expected Impact of Ad Extensions:** Adding sitelinks, callouts, call extensions, structured snippets, etc., improves ad visibility and CTR, raising Ad Rank.

**Step 3: Second-Price Charging (2 marks)**
Google charges the advertiser only the **minimum amount** required to beat the next competitor's Ad Rank, plus ₹0.01. This incentivizes advertisers to bid their true value while rewarding relevance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A small advertiser with a high Quality Score (e.g., QS=9) bidding ₹40 can beat a competitor with QS=4 bidding ₹80, because $40 \times 9 = 360 > 80 \times 4 = 320$.

---

#### Part A(b) — Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate CTR**
$$\text{CTR} = \frac{2{,}400}{60{,}000} \times 100 = 4\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate CPC**
$$\text{CPC} = \frac{36{,}000}{2{,}400} = \text{₹}15$$

**Step 3: Calculate Conversion Rate (CVR)**
$$\text{CVR} = \frac{120}{2{,}400} \times 100 = 5\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate CPA**
$$\text{CPA} = \frac{36{,}000}{120} = \text{₹}300$$

**Step 5: Calculate ROAS**
$$\text{ROAS} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{36{,}000} \times 100 = 333.33\%$$

**Step 6: Profitability Commentary (2 marks)**
The ROAS of **333.33%** means ₹3.33 returned per ₹1 spent. The campaign is **highly profitable** provided the gross margin on books exceeds 30% (since $1/3.33 = 30\%$). For an online bookstore, this is an excellent return, and the campaign should be **scaled** by increasing daily budget.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the structural hierarchy of a Google Ads account. Discuss any five key PPC metrics used to evaluate campaign performance, with their formulas and industry benchmarks.**

---

#### Part B(a) — Account Hierarchy (7 Marks)

**Step 1: The Three-Tier Architecture (3 marks)**
A Google Ads account follows a strict **Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Keyword/Ad** hierarchy, as shown in the diagram below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[ACCOUNT] --> N0[CAMPAIGN]
    N0 --> O0[AD GROUP]
    O0 --> P0[KEYWORDS]
    O0 --> Q0[ADS]
    O0 --> R0[LANDING PAGES]

    style M0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style N0 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style O0 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

**Step 2: Functional Description of Each Layer (4 marks)**
- **Account Level:** Holds billing information, user access, and links to Google Analytics.
- **Campaign Level:** Defines the daily/monthly budget, geographic targeting, language, ad network (Search/Display/Shopping), and bidding strategy.
- **Ad Group Level:** A logical bucket containing a small, tightly themed set of keywords (5–20 keywords is best practice) and 2–3 variations of ad copy.
- **Keyword/Ad Level:** The actual match types (Broad, Phrase, Exact, Negative) and creative assets (headlines, descriptions, sitelinks).

**Best Practice:** Keep ad groups small and tightly themed to maximize Quality Score and improve ad relevance.

---

#### Part B(b) — Five Key PPC Metrics (7 Marks)

| # | Metric | Formula | Industry Benchmark | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CTR** | $\dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Search: 2%–5%; Display: 0.5%–1% | Measures ad attractiveness |
| 2 | **CPC** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Clicks}}$ | ₹5–₹100 (varies by industry) | Measures cost efficiency |
| 3 | **CPA** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Conversions}}$ | Industry-specific | Measures customer acquisition cost |
| 4 | **ROAS** | $\dfrac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | 4:1 (400%) is "good" | Measures revenue efficiency |
| 5 | **Quality Score** | Proprietary 1–10 | 7+ is excellent | Measures ad-to-landing-page relevance |

> **Conclusion:** These five metrics together provide a **360° view** of a PPC campaign: CTR for creative, CPC for cost, CVR for landing page, CPA for acquisition, and ROAS for profitability. A balanced optimization strategy monitors all five.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PPC questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CTR base:** CTR is Clicks ÷ Impressions. Conversion Rate is Conversions ÷ Clicks. Mixing these bases is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting × 100:** Formulas like CTR, CVR, and ROAS need a ×100 multiplier to express as percentages. Examiners explicitly look for the percentage format.
> 3. **Ad Rank confusion:** Students often write "highest bid wins" — this is **wrong**. The Ad Rank is bid × Quality Score. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this misconception.
> 4. **No unit on numerical answers:** Always write "₹20 per click" or "3%" — bare numbers without units lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the Ad Rank derivation:** If asked to "explain the auction mechanism," simply stating the formula is **incomplete**. You must also explain the second-price charging principle.
> 6. **Confusing PPC with SEO:** PPC is paid and immediate; SEO is organic and slow. Examiners test this distinction in 3-mark and 7-mark questions alike.
> 7. **Quality Score components not listed:** If asked "What is Quality Score?", you must list the **three components**: Expected CTR, Ad Relevance, and Landing Page Experience. Skipping any one costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ PPC = Pay Per Click; payment is triggered **only on click**, not on view.
- ✅ The three main PPC platforms: **Google Ads, Microsoft Ads (Bing), Meta Ads**.
- ✅ PPC is a subset of **Search Engine Marketing (SEM)**; SEO is its organic counterpart.
- ✅ **CTR** = (Clicks / Impressions) × 100 — search benchmark: 2%–5%.
- ✅ **CPC** = Total Cost / Clicks — what you actually pay per click.
- ✅ **CPM** = (Total Cost / Impressions) × 1000 — useful for awareness campaigns.
- ✅ **CPA** = Total Cost / Conversions — true cost of customer acquisition.
- ✅ **ROAS** = (Revenue / Ad Spend) × 100 — profitability metric; 4:1 (400%) is the industry "good" target.
- ✅ **Conversion Rate (CVR)** = (Conversions / Clicks) × 100 — measures landing page effectiveness.
- ✅ **Ad Rank = Max CPC Bid × Quality Score × Ad Extension Impact** — the core formula.
- ✅ **Quality Score** has three components: Expected CTR, Ad Relevance, Landing Page Experience.
- ✅ Google uses a **second-price auction** — you pay just enough to beat the competitor + ₹0.01.
- ✅ **Account hierarchy:** Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Keywords/Ads → Landing Page.
- ✅ PPC advantages: **immediate results, measurable, scalable, intent-driven**.
- ✅ PPC disadvantages: **costly over time, click fraud risk, requires continuous optimization**.
- ✅ Ad Extensions (sitelinks, callouts, structured snippets) **boost Ad Rank** at no extra cost.
- ✅ Negative Keywords prevent ads from showing for irrelevant searches, **saving budget**.
- ✅ PPC is best for **direct response goals**: sales, leads, app installs, signups.
- ✅ Remember the formula for budget: $\text{Total Budget} = \text{Clicks} \times \text{Avg. CPC}$.

> **Best of luck for your KTU Module 3 exam! 🚀**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET833 - DIGITAL MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Google AdWords

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Google AdWords** (rebranded as **Google Ads** in 2018) is Google's flagship online advertising platform that enables businesses to display brief advertisements, service offerings, product listings, and video content within the Google ad network to web users. It operates on a **Pay-Per-Click (PPC)** auction-based model where advertisers bid on specific **keywords** to have their clickable ads appear in Google's search results, partner websites, YouTube, and mobile applications.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Smart Newspaper Stand**
> 
> Imagine a busy marketplace where a newspaper vendor has 3 display racks (top, middle, bottom) for ads. Traditionally, the **richest advertiser** gets the top rack. Google AdWords flips this: the vendor doesn't just look at how much money you have — he looks at **how relevant your ad is** to what shoppers are actually searching for.
> 
> Think of Google AdWords like **booking a billboard on a highway, but only paying when a car actually stops to look at it**. The billboard is "smart" — it appears only to people searching for your exact product, in your service area, at the right time. If no one clicks, you pay nothing. This makes it a **performance-based** advertising system, radically different from traditional media (TV, Radio, Print) where you pay for exposure regardless of results.

### 1.2 Core Metrics & Terminology (Bolded Highlights)

- **CTR (Click-Through Rate):** The percentage of users who click your ad after seeing it.
- **CPC (Cost Per Click):** The actual price you pay for each click on your advertisement.
- **CPM (Cost Per Mille):** Cost per one thousand impressions (views) — used mainly for Display campaigns.
- **CPA (Cost Per Acquisition):** The cost to acquire one customer (a conversion).
- **Quality Score (QS):** Google's 1–10 rating of the quality and relevance of your keywords and ads.
- **Ad Rank:** The position your ad occupies on the search engine results page (SERP).
- **Conversion Rate (CVR):** The percentage of clicks that result in a desired action (purchase, sign-up).
- **Impression Share (IS):** The percentage of eligible impressions your ad actually received.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In 3-mark questions, students often confuse **CPC** with **CPM**. Remember: **CPC = pay per CLICK**, **CPM = pay per 1000 views (MILLE = thousand in Latin)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why & How" of Google AdWords

Google AdWords is built on **three foundational pillars** that determine whether your ad shows, where it shows, and how much you pay:

#### Pillar 1: The Auction System
- Every time a user performs a Google search, an **instant auction** is triggered.
- The Google Ads system evaluates all eligible advertisers competing for that keyword.
- The auction is **not** won by the highest bidder alone — it is a hybrid of **Bid × Quality Score**.

#### Pillar 2: Quality Score (QS)
- Quality Score is calculated on a scale of **1 to 10** (10 being the best).
- It is determined by three factors:
  1. **Expected CTR** — How likely users are to click your ad.
  2. **Ad Relevance** — How closely your ad matches the user's search intent.
  3. **Landing Page Experience** — How useful, relevant, and fast your destination webpage is.

#### Pillar 3: The Bidding Mechanism
- Advertisers set a **maximum bid** — the highest they are *willing* to pay per click.
- However, the **actual CPC charged** is often lower than the maximum bid (second-price auction logic).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Examination**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Description |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Ad Rank | $\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Max CPC Bid} \times \text{Quality Score}$ | Dimensionless; determines ad position |
| 2 | Actual CPC (Second-Price Auction) | $\text{CPC} = \dfrac{(\text{Ad Rank of next bidder below you} + \$0.01)}{\text{Your Quality Score}}$ | In monetary units (\$) |
| 3 | Click-Through Rate (CTR) | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Clicks}}{\text{Number of Impressions}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 4 | Conversion Rate (CVR) | $\text{CVR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Conversions}}{\text{Number of Clicks}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 5 | Cost Per Acquisition (CPA) | $\text{CPA} = \dfrac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{Number of Conversions}}$ | In monetary units (\$) |
| 6 | Cost Per Mille (CPM) | $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Number of Impressions}} \times 1000$ | In monetary units per 1000 impressions |
| 7 | Return on Ad Spend (ROAS) | $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 8 | Impression Share | $\text{IS} = \dfrac{\text{Impressions Received}}{\text{Total Eligible Impressions}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Valuation:** In your answer sheets, write **CTR = (Clicks / Impressions) × 100**, NOT using the `$\vert$` symbol inside table cells. The above table uses `\vert`-free formatting to prevent rendering errors.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Google AdWords

- **Search Engine Marketing (SEM):** Primary tool for paid search visibility.
- **E-commerce Sales:** Drives qualified traffic with high purchase intent.
- **Lead Generation:** B2B companies use Search and Display ads to capture qualified leads.
- **Brand Awareness:** Display & Video campaigns boost visibility even without direct clicks.
- **Local Business Promotion:** Geo-targeted ads drive foot traffic to physical stores.
- **Remarketing:** Targets users who previously visited your website but did not convert.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Question banks frequently test the **Ad Rank formula** and the **second-price auction CPC calculation**. Memorize formula row #1 and #2 above — they are worth 7 marks each in long-answer questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ad Rank Position Logic

The Ad Rank determines where your ad appears on the SERP. Let us derive the position logic:

**Step 1: Identify the bidders.**
Suppose three advertisers (A, B, C) bid on the keyword "digital marketing course Kerala" with the following parameters:

| Advertiser | Max CPC Bid (\$) | Quality Score (1–10) |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| A | 5.00 | 7 |
| B | 4.00 | 9 |
| C | 3.00 | 8 |

**Step 2: Apply the Ad Rank formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ad Rank}_A &= \text{Bid}_A \times \text{QS}_A \\
&= 5.00 \times 7 \\
&= 35
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ad Rank}_B &= \text{Bid}_B \times \text{QS}_B \\
&= 4.00 \times 9 \\
&= 36
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ad Rank}_C &= \text{Bid}_C \times \text{QS}_C \\
&= 3.00 \times 8 \\
&= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Rank the advertisers in descending order of Ad Rank.**

> **Final Ranking:** Position 1 = B (36), Position 2 = A (35), Position 3 = C (24)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight:** Notice that Advertiser B wins the top position despite bidding **\$1 LESS** than A. This demonstrates the core strength of Google's auction: **relevance beats raw money**. A higher Quality Score effectively gives you a "discount" on your bid.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Actual CPC (Second-Price Auction Logic)

The actual CPC you pay is determined by the Ad Rank of the **competitor immediately below you**, plus \$0.01, divided by **your** Quality Score.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Using the same three advertisers above, let us calculate what Advertiser A (Position 2) actually pays per click:

**Step 1: Identify the Ad Rank of the next competitor below you.**
Advertiser A is in Position 2. The next competitor below A is Advertiser C with Ad Rank = 24.

**Step 2: Add \$0.01 (the minimum increment to outbid).**

$$
\text{Competitor's Threshold} = 24 + 0.01 = 24.01
$$

**Step 3: Divide by your Quality Score to find the Actual CPC.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Actual CPC}_A &= \dfrac{24.01}{\text{QS}_A} \\
&= \dfrac{24.01}{7} \\
&= 3.43
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Advertiser A's maximum bid was **\$5.00**, but they only pay **\$3.43** per click — a **31.4% savings** purely due to good Quality Score optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Formula Derivation:**
> 
> $$
> \text{Actual CPC} = \dfrac{(\text{Ad Rank of next bidder} + \$0.01)}{\text{Your Quality Score}}
> $$
> 
> This is mathematically derived from the constraint that your **Ad Rank** must be **just barely higher** than the next bidder's. Solving for CPC gives the above expression.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of CTR and Conversion Rate Relationships

**Step 1: Calculate the relationship between spend, clicks, and conversions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Clicks} &= \text{Impressions} \times \text{CTR} \\
\text{Total Conversions} &= \text{Total Clicks} \times \text{CVR} \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{Total Clicks} \times \text{CPC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Combine these to derive CPA in terms of micro-metrics.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPA} &= \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Conversions}} \\
&= \dfrac{\text{Total Clicks} \times \text{CPC}}{\text{Total Clicks} \times \text{CVR}} \\
&= \dfrac{\text{CPC}}{\text{CVR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** This simplification ($\text{CPA} = \text{CPC} / \text{CVR}$) shows that to **lower CPA**, a marketer must either **reduce CPC** (improve Quality Score) or **increase CVR** (improve landing page experience). It is a fundamental optimization trade-off in digital marketing analytics.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — End-to-End KTU Style

**Problem:** An e-commerce brand in Kerala runs a Google AdWords campaign for the keyword "buy sarees online." Data for one month:

- Impressions: **100,000**
- Clicks: **2,000**
- Conversions (purchases): **50**
- Total Ad Spend: **₹20,000**
- Revenue Generated: **₹75,000**

**Step 1: Calculate CTR.**

$$
\text{CTR} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 100 = 2\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate CPC.**

$$
\text{CPC} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{2{,}000} = \text{₹ } 10
$$

**Step 3: Calculate CVR.**

$$
\text{CVR} = \dfrac{50}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 2.5\%
$$

**Step 4: Calculate CPA.**

$$
\text{CPA} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{50} = \text{₹ } 400
$$

**Step 5: Calculate ROAS.**

$$
\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{20{,}000} \times 100 = 375\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For every ₹1 spent, the brand earned **₹3.75** in revenue. The campaign is profitable since ROAS exceeds 100%. A campaign with ROAS < 100% is **value-destructive**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Google Ads Account Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Google Ads Account] --> B[Campaign Level]
    B --> C1[Campaign 1: Search]
    B --> C2[Campaign 2: Display]
    B --> C3[Campaign 3: Shopping]
    C1 --> D1[Ad Group 1: Branded Keywords]
    C1 --> D2[Ad Group 2: Generic Keywords]
    C1 --> D3[Ad Group 3: Competitor Keywords]
    D1 --> E1[Keyword: Buy Sarees Online]
    D1 --> E2[Keyword: Kerala Saree Shop]
    D1 --> F1[Ad Copy 1: Headline + Description]
    D1 --> F2[Ad Copy 2: Headline + Description]
    D1 --> G1[Landing Page URL]
    D2 --> E3[Keyword: Saree Offers]
    D2 --> E4[Keyword: Discount Sarees]
    D2 --> F3[Ad Copy 3: Headline + Description]
    D2 --> G2[Landing Page URL]
    D3 --> E5[Keyword: Competitor Brand Name]
    D3 --> F4[Ad Copy 4: Headline + Description]
    D3 --> G3[Landing Page URL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** The hierarchy follows a strict 4-tier model: **Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Keyword/Ad**. KTU questions often test this hierarchy in 3-mark questions. Each level has its own settings (budget at campaign level, bids at ad group level, etc.).

---

### 4.2 The Google Ads Auction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[User enters search query] --> P1[Google triggers instant auction]
    P1 --> P2[Filter advertisers by keyword eligibility]
    P2 --> P3[Calculate Ad Rank for each advertiser]
    P3 --> P4[Ad Rank equals Bid times Quality Score]
    P4 --> P5[Rank advertisers from highest to lowest Ad Rank]
    P5 --> P6[Only ads above minimum threshold are shown]
    P6 --> P7[User sees ads on SERP]
    P7 --> P8[User clicks an ad]
    P8 --> P9[Advertiser charged Actual CPC]
    P9 --> P10[Conversion tracking if user converts]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** This flow diagram represents the canonical answer for the 14-mark question *"Explain the working of Google AdWords auction system"*. Memorize the sequence: **Query → Auction → Eligibility Filter → Ad Rank Calculation → Ranking → Display → Click → Charge**.

---

### 4.3 Quality Score Components Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QS[Quality Score 1 to 10] --> CTR[Expected CTR]
    QS --> AR[Ad Relevance]
    QS --> LPEX[Landing Page Experience]
    CTR --> CTR1[Historical performance]
    CTR --> CTR2[Expected click probability]
    AR --> AR1[Keyword to ad copy match]
    AR --> AR2[Search intent alignment]
    LPEX --> LP1[Page load speed]
    LPEX --> LP2[Mobile friendliness]
    LPEX --> LP3[Content relevance]
    LPEX --> LP4[Transparency and trust signals]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A Quality Score of **7 or above** is considered "Good" by Google. A score of **5 or below** results in higher CPCs and lower ad positions. Students frequently lose marks by failing to mention all **three** components — CTR, Ad Relevance, and Landing Page Experience.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define Google AdWords. List any **two key features** of the Google AdWords platform.

**Model Answer:**

Google AdWords (now Google Ads) is Google's online advertising platform that displays clickable ads in Google search results, partner websites, YouTube, and mobile applications. It operates on a **Pay-Per-Click (PPC)** model, where advertisers bid on keywords and pay only when a user clicks on their ad.

**Two Key Features:**
1. **Keyword-Based Targeting:** Ads are shown to users based on the keywords they search, ensuring high relevance and intent alignment.
2. **Pay-Per-Click Pricing Model:** Advertisers are charged only when a user clicks the ad, not for mere views or impressions (in Search campaigns).
3. *(Optional third)* **Real-Time Analytics:** Comprehensive dashboards provide CTR, CPC, conversion tracking, and ROI metrics.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **CPC** and **CPM** bidding strategies in Google AdWords.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CPC (Cost Per Click) | CPM (Cost Per Mille) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Full Form | Cost Per Click | Cost Per Mille (per 1000 impressions) |
| Payment Trigger | Advertiser pays when user clicks the ad | Advertiser pays for every 1000 ad views (impressions) |
| Best For | Performance campaigns, lead generation, sales | Brand awareness, reach campaigns |
| Common Network | Search Network | Display Network, YouTube |
| Formula | $\text{CPC} = \text{Total Cost} / \text{Total Clicks}$ | $\text{CPM} = (\text{Total Cost} / \text{Impressions}) \times 1000$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q3. (a)** Explain in detail the **working of the Google AdWords auction system** with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Google AdWords auction system is a real-time, automated process that determines which ads appear, in what order, and how much the advertiser pays. The detailed working is as follows:

1. **User Query Input:** When a user types a search query into Google, the system instantly checks whether any advertisers have bid on keywords matching that query.
2. **Eligibility Filter:** Advertisers whose keywords match the search query (and who meet policy/budget criteria) enter the auction pool.
3. **Ad Rank Calculation:** For each eligible advertiser, Google calculates the **Ad Rank** using the formula:
   $$\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Max CPC Bid} \times \text{Quality Score}$$
4. **Ranking:** All eligible ads are sorted in descending order of Ad Rank. The highest Ad Rank gets Position 1, second-highest gets Position 2, and so on.
5. **Threshold Filtering:** Ads that do not meet a minimum Ad Rank threshold are not shown at all.
6. **Display:** Eligible ads are displayed on the SERP with their position, headline, description, and URL.
7. **Click and Charge:** If the user clicks an ad, the advertiser is charged an **Actual CPC** calculated as:
   $$\text{Actual CPC} = \dfrac{(\text{Ad Rank of next lower bidder} + \$0.01)}{\text{Your Quality Score}}$$

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 (Auction Process Flow) of these notes.

**Conclusion:** The auction system ensures that **relevance is rewarded over budget size**, benefiting both users (better ads) and quality-conscious advertisers (lower costs).

---

**(b)** Three advertisers A, B, and C bid on the keyword "best MBA colleges in Kerala" with the following data: A has Bid = ₹40, QS = 6; B has Bid = ₹30, QS = 9; C has Bid = ₹25, QS = 7. Calculate the **Ad Rank** of each and determine the **Actual CPC** paid by the advertiser in Position 1. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate Ad Rank for each advertiser.**

$$
\text{Ad Rank}_A = 40 \times 6 = 240
$$

$$
\text{Ad Rank}_B = 30 \times 9 = 270
$$

$$
\text{Ad Rank}_C = 25 \times 7 = 175
$$

**Step 2: Rank in descending order.**

- **Position 1:** Advertiser B (Ad Rank = 270)
- **Position 2:** Advertiser A (Ad Rank = 240)
- **Position 3:** Advertiser C (Ad Rank = 175)

**Step 3: Calculate Actual CPC for Position 1 (Advertiser B).**

The next advertiser below B is A, with Ad Rank = 240.

$$
\text{Actual CPC}_B = \dfrac{(\text{Ad Rank of A} + 0.01)}{\text{Quality Score of B}}
$$

$$
\text{Actual CPC}_B = \dfrac{240 + 0.01}{9} = \dfrac{240.01}{9} = 26.67
$$

> **Final Answer:** Advertiser B pays **₹26.67 per click** despite having a maximum bid of ₹30. This illustrates the **second-price auction** mechanism where the actual CPC is always less than the bid due to Quality Score influence.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q4. (a)** Explain the **components of Quality Score** in Google AdWords. How does improving Quality Score reduce advertising costs? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Quality Score Components:**

1. **Expected Click-Through Rate (CTR):** Google's prediction of how likely users are to click your ad when shown. It is influenced by historical performance of the ad, keyword, and similar ads in your account.
2. **Ad Relevance:** Measures how closely your ad copy (headline + description) matches the user's search intent. Google compares the keyword, ad text, and landing page for topical consistency.
3. **Landing Page Experience:** Evaluates the usefulness and relevance of the webpage users land on. Factors include page load speed, mobile-friendliness, content quality, navigation, and transparency (e.g., clear contact information, privacy policy).

**How Improving Quality Score Reduces Costs:**

From the Ad Rank formula:
$$\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Max CPC Bid} \times \text{Quality Score}$$

A higher Quality Score allows an advertiser to achieve a higher Ad Rank with a **lower bid**. This directly impacts Actual CPC:

$$\text{Actual CPC} = \dfrac{\text{Ad Rank of next bidder} + \$0.01}{\text{Your Quality Score}}$$

> **Numerical Illustration:** If Quality Score increases from 5 to 9 (a 1.8× improvement), the Actual CPC decreases by approximately **44%** for the same ad position. Additionally, higher QS leads to better ad positions, more clicks, and improved conversion rates, all of which lower the overall **Cost Per Acquisition (CPA)**.

---

**(b)** An online bookstore runs a Google AdWords campaign with the following monthly data:
- Total Impressions: **50,000**
- Total Clicks: **1,500**
- Total Conversions (orders): **75**
- Total Ad Spend: **₹15,000**
- Total Revenue: **₹45,000**

Calculate the **CTR, CPC, CVR, CPA, and ROAS**. Is the campaign profitable? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate CTR (Click-Through Rate).**

$$
\text{CTR} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 3.0\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate CPC (Cost Per Click).**

$$
\text{CPC} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{1{,}500} = \text{₹ } 10
$$

**Step 3: Calculate CVR (Conversion Rate).**

$$
\text{CVR} = \dfrac{75}{1{,}500} \times 100 = 5.0\%
$$

**Step 4: Calculate CPA (Cost Per Acquisition).**

$$
\text{CPA} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{75} = \text{₹ } 200
$$

**Step 5: Calculate ROAS (Return on Ad Spend).**

$$
\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{45{,}000}{15{,}000} \times 100 = 300\%
$$

**Profitability Analysis:**

Since ROAS = **300% > 100%**, the campaign is **highly profitable**. For every ₹1 spent on advertising, the bookstore earns **₹3 in revenue**. The CPA of ₹200 per order is also significantly lower than the average order value (₹45,000 / 75 = ₹600 per order), indicating a healthy profit margin per transaction.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the +₹0.01 / +$0.01 increment** in the second-price auction CPC formula. The literal "+ 0.01" term is mandatory — omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Ad Rank with Bid:** Ad Rank is NOT the bid amount. It is the **product** of Bid and Quality Score. Writing "Ad Rank = Bid" will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up CTR and CVR:** CTR is Clicks/Impressions. CVR is Conversions/Clicks. Students frequently interchange these.
> 4. **Not mentioning all three Quality Score components** (Expected CTR, Ad Relevance, Landing Page Experience). Listing only two will lose 2 marks out of 7.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert to percentage:** CTR and CVR must be expressed with the **× 100** multiplier. A value of 0.03 is wrong; **3%** is correct.
> 6. **Skipping the units in CPC/CPA answers:** Always write "₹10 per click" or "₹200 per acquisition" — not just the number.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Google AdWords**
> 
> - ✅ Google AdWords is Google's **Pay-Per-Click (PPC)** advertising platform (rebranded as Google Ads in 2018).
> - ✅ The core formula is **Ad Rank = Max CPC Bid × Quality Score** — memorise this verbatim.
> - ✅ Quality Score has **3 components**: Expected CTR, Ad Relevance, Landing Page Experience.
> - ✅ Actual CPC follows the **second-price auction** logic: $\text{CPC} = (\text{Ad Rank of next bidder} + 0.01) / \text{Your QS}$.
> - ✅ **CTR** is Clicks/Impressions; **CVR** is Conversions/Clicks; **CPA** is Total Cost/Conversions.
> - ✅ **ROAS > 100%** means the campaign is profitable; **ROAS < 100%** means it is loss-making.
> - ✅ Account structure hierarchy: **Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Keywords/Ads**.
> - ✅ Campaign types: **Search, Display, Shopping, Video, App, Smart** — each with its own bidding best practices.
> - ✅ Higher Quality Score = **lower CPC** + **better ad position** + **higher ROI**.
> - ✅ Remarketing, Ad Extensions (Sitelinks, Callouts, Call Extensions), and Audience Targeting are advanced optimization levers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bidding Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET833 - DIGITAL MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bidding Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bidding Strategy** is a set of rules and algorithms used by advertisers in Pay-Per-Click (PPC) and programmatic advertising platforms (such as Google Ads, Meta Ads, and programmatic DSPs) to determine **how much they are willing to pay for each user action** — whether that action is a click, impression, view, or conversion.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, bidding strategies are classified into two broad categories:

1. **Manual Bidding** — where the advertiser sets individual keyword, ad group, or campaign-level bid amounts.
2. **Automated (Smart) Bidding** — where machine learning algorithms in the ad platform set bids at auction time based on real-time signals (device, location, time of day, audience, browser, OS).

The **fundamental formula governing the auction** is:

$$\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Bid Amount} \times \text{Quality Score} \times \text{Expected Impact of Ad Extensions}$$

The advertiser only pays the minimum amount required to beat the competitor below them:

$$\text{Actual CPC} = \left(\frac{\text{Ad Rank of Competitor Below}}{\text{Your Quality Score}}\right) + \$0.01$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Auction House"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Imagine a Silent Art Auction:**
>
> You walk into an auction hall where 10 paintings are being sold simultaneously. Every bidder whispers a secret maximum price they are willing to pay. The auctioneer (Google's algorithm) considers:
> - **How much you bid** (your Bid Amount)
> - **How prestigious your gallery is** (your Quality Score, based on CTR, ad relevance, landing page experience)
> - **How well-decorated your painting frame is** (Ad Extensions)
>
> The painting goes to the highest combined score, **but** you only pay just enough to outbid the runner-up (the "second-price auction" model). If you are willing to bid ₹500 but only need ₹210 to win, you pay ₹210 — this protects advertisers from overpaying.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bidding (Bold for Emphasis)

| Metric | Full Form | Definition |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| **CPC** | Cost Per Click | Amount paid per ad click |
| **CPA** | Cost Per Acquisition | Cost incurred per conversion |
| **ROAS** | Return On Ad Spend | Revenue earned per rupee spent on ads |
| **CPM** | Cost Per Mille | Cost per 1,000 impressions |
| **CPV** | Cost Per View | Cost per video view |
| **CTR** | Click-Through Rate | % of impressions that result in clicks |
| **QS** | Quality Score | Google's 1–10 rating of keyword/ad/landing page |

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview — Types of Bidding Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3 Highlight:**
> Students must be able to differentiate between **manual** and **automated** bidding, and select an appropriate strategy based on campaign objectives (awareness, consideration, conversion).

**Quick Categorization Tree:**

- **A. Manual Bidding**
  - Manual CPC
  - Viewable CPM (vCPM)
  - CPV Bidding

- **B. Automated Bidding (Smart Bidding)**
  - Maximize Clicks
  - Maximize Conversions
  - Target CPA
  - Target ROAS
  - Enhanced CPC
  - Maximize Conversion Value
  - Target Impression Share
  - Cost Per View (CPV)

---

## 1.5 When to Use What — Strategic Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Industry Standard):**
> - **Awareness campaigns** → Target Impression Share / vCPM
> - **Traffic campaigns** → Maximize Clicks / Manual CPC
> - **Lead generation** → Target CPA / Maximize Conversions
> - **E-commerce / Sales** → Target ROAS / Maximize Conversion Value
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Auction Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

Every time a user performs a search on Google or browses a Display Network site, an **ad auction** occurs in less than a millisecond. The auction follows this sequence:

- **Step 1 — Eligibility Check**: The platform checks if your ad is eligible to appear for the query (e.g., keyword match, budget, policy compliance).
- **Step 2 — Bid Filtering**: Bids below the platform's minimum threshold are excluded.
- **Step 3 — Ad Rank Computation**: For each eligible ad, the Ad Rank is calculated using the formula:

$$\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Max Bid} \times \text{Quality Score} \times \text{Expected Impact of Extensions}$$

- **Step 4 — Ranking**: Ads are ranked in descending order of Ad Rank.
- **Step 5 — Pricing (Second-Price Auction)**: Each winner pays the minimum bid needed to be ranked above the next competitor.
- **Step 6 — Display**: The winning ads are shown to the user.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Bidding Strategy

### 2.2.1 Manual CPC Bidding

- Advertiser sets the **maximum cost-per-click** they are willing to pay for each keyword.
- Offers **maximum control** but requires constant monitoring.
- Best for: Small budgets, learning phase, niche campaigns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters:** Manual CPC is the foundational bidding model. All automated strategies evolved from it. Even in 2024, KTU examiners frequently ask about Manual CPC as a baseline.

### 2.2.2 Enhanced CPC (eCPC)

- A **semi-automated** hybrid model.
- The advertiser sets a Manual CPC, but the platform can adjust the bid **up or down by up to 30%** based on the likelihood of conversion.
- Best for: Advertisers who want manual control with automation assistance.

### 2.2.3 Maximize Clicks

- Fully automated strategy that **sets bids to get the maximum number of clicks** within the daily budget.
- Uses historical data to identify click-worthy auctions.
- Best for: Driving website traffic, brand awareness top-of-funnel.

### 2.2.4 Maximize Conversions

- The algorithm bids to **maximize the total number of conversions** within the budget.
- No target CPA is set — the system optimizes for raw conversion count.
- Best for: Lead generation when flexibility on cost is acceptable.

### 2.2.5 Target CPA (tCPA)

- Advertiser specifies a **target cost-per-acquisition**.
- The algorithm adjusts bids in real-time to achieve as many conversions as possible at that target cost.
- Best for: Performance marketers with clear CPA benchmarks.

### 2.2.6 Target ROAS (tROAS)

- Advertiser specifies a **target return on ad spend** (e.g., 400% = ₹4 revenue per ₹1 spent).
- Algorithm prioritizes auctions likely to deliver the highest revenue.
- Best for: E-commerce businesses with clear revenue tracking.

### 2.2.7 Maximize Conversion Value

- Optimizes for **total conversion value (revenue)**, not just conversion count.
- Best for: E-commerce with variable product margins.

### 2.2.8 Target Impression Share

- Advertiser specifies what **percentage of impressions** they want to capture (e.g., appear in 80% of eligible auctions).
- Useful for **brand defense** and competitive conquesting.
- Best for: Brand awareness, top-of-page positioning.

### 2.2.9 CPM / vCPM Bidding

- Bid based on **cost per thousand impressions** (or viewable impressions).
- Used in Display & Video campaigns.
- Best for: Awareness, reach, brand recall.

### 2.2.10 CPV Bidding (Cost Per View)

- Used in **video campaigns** (YouTube).
- Advertiser pays when a user watches 30 seconds (or the full ad if shorter) or interacts.
- Best for: Video engagement campaigns.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $CTR = \dfrac{Clicks}{Impressions} \times 100$ | Click-Through Rate | Percentage (%) |
| 2 | $CPC = \dfrac{Total\ Cost}{Total\ Clicks}$ | Cost Per Click | Currency (₹, $) |
| 3 | $CPA = \dfrac{Total\ Cost}{Total\ Conversions}$ | Cost Per Acquisition | Currency (₹, $) |
| 4 | $ROAS = \dfrac{Revenue\ Generated}{Ad\ Spend} \times 100$ | Return On Ad Spend | Percentage (%) |
| 5 | $CPM = \dfrac{Total\ Cost}{Impressions} \times 1000$ | Cost Per Mille | Currency / 1000 imps |
| 6 | $Conv.\ Rate = \dfrac{Conversions}{Clicks} \times 100$ | Conversion Rate | Percentage (%) |
| 7 | $Ad\ Rank = Bid \times QS \times Extensions$ | Auction Rank | Score |
| 8 | $Actual\ CPC = \dfrac{Competitor\ Ad\ Rank}{Your\ QS} + \$0.01$ | Second-Price Payment | Currency |
| 9 | $Profit = Revenue - Ad\ Spend$ | Net Profit | Currency |
| 10 | $Effective\ Bid = Manual\ CPC \times (1 \pm 30\%)$ | eCPC Range | Currency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Exams:** Memorize Formulas 1 through 8. These appear in nearly every Digital Marketing exam paper under Module 3.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

- **E-commerce Platforms** (Amazon, Flipkart) use Target ROAS to scale profitably.
- **SaaS Companies** use Target CPA to optimize lead-generation funnels.
- **D2C Brands** combine Maximize Conversions + Bid Adjustments for hyper-local targeting.
- **Startups** rely on Manual CPC initially, then graduate to Smart Bidding once they accumulate 30+ conversions/month (Google's minimum threshold for tCPA/tROAS).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The bidding optimization problem is essentially a **constrained optimization problem** in Operations Research. It can be modeled as:

$$\max_{b \in B} \sum_{i=1}^{n} f(b_i)\ \ \text{subject to}\ \ \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \leq \text{Budget}$$

where $f(b_i)$ is the conversion function at bid level $b_i$ for keyword $i$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Numerical Problem — CPC Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
An advertiser runs a Google Search campaign. In one week, the campaign generated:
- Impressions: 100,000
- Clicks: 2,500
- Cost: ₹18,750
- Conversions: 125

**Calculate:** (a) CTR, (b) CPC, (c) Conversion Rate, (d) CPA.

---

### 3.1.1 Solution — Part (a): Click-Through Rate (CTR)

Using the formula:

$$CTR = \frac{Clicks}{Impressions} \times 100$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the given values into the formula.

$$CTR = \frac{2{,}500}{100{,}000} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Perform the division.

$$CTR = 0.025 \times 100$$

**Step 3:** Compute the final percentage value.

$$\boxed{CTR = 2.5\%}$$

**Interpretation:** A CTR of 2.5% is considered **above average** for Search campaigns (industry benchmark ≈ 3–5% for top positions). The ad creative and keyword relevance are working well.

---

### 3.1.2 Solution — Part (b): Cost Per Click (CPC)

Using the formula:

$$CPC = \frac{Total\ Cost}{Total\ Clicks}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the values.

$$CPC = \frac{₹18{,}750}{2{,}500}$$

**Step 2:** Perform the division.

$$\boxed{CPC = ₹7.50\ per\ click}$$

**Interpretation:** Each click costs the advertiser ₹7.50. This is reasonable for competitive industries like insurance, legal, or education.

---

### 3.1.3 Solution — Part (c): Conversion Rate

Using the formula:

$$Conversion\ Rate = \frac{Conversions}{Clicks} \times 100$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the values.

$$Conversion\ Rate = \frac{125}{2{,}500} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Compute the division.

$$Conversion\ Rate = 0.05 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{Conversion\ Rate = 5\%}$$

**Interpretation:** 1 in every 20 clicks converts. For a B2C campaign, this is excellent (industry average: 2–3%).

---

### 3.1.4 Solution — Part (d): Cost Per Acquisition (CPA)

Using the formula:

$$CPA = \frac{Total\ Cost}{Total\ Conversions}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the values.

$$CPA = \frac{₹18{,}750}{125}$$

**Step 2:** Perform the division.

$$\boxed{CPA = ₹150\ per\ conversion}$$

**Interpretation:** Acquiring each customer costs ₹150. If the average order value is ₹600, the advertiser breaks even on first purchase.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem — ROAS & Profitability

**Problem Statement:**
An e-commerce store spends ₹50,000 on Google Ads in one month and generates ₹2,50,000 in revenue from those ads.
**Calculate:** (a) ROAS, (b) Profit (assume product COGS = 40% of revenue).

---

### 3.2.1 Solution — Part (a): ROAS

Using the formula:

$$ROAS = \frac{Revenue}{Ad\ Spend} \times 100$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the values.

$$ROAS = \frac{₹2{,}50{,}000}{₹50{,}000} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Perform the division.

$$ROAS = 5 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{ROAS = 500\%}$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹1 spent, the advertiser earns ₹5 in revenue. This is the **break-even ROAS** when COGS = 60% of revenue.

---

### 3.2.2 Solution — Part (b): Net Profit

**Step 1:** Calculate COGS.

$$COGS = 40\% \times ₹2{,}50{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Gross Profit (before ad cost).

$$Gross\ Profit = Revenue - COGS = ₹2{,}50{,}000 - ₹1{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Net Profit (after ad cost).

$$Net\ Profit = Gross\ Profit - Ad\ Spend = ₹1{,}50{,}000 - ₹50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{Net\ Profit = ₹1{,}00{,}000}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem — Bid Optimization Using Quality Score

**Problem Statement:**
Advertiser A has a Max CPC Bid of ₹20 and Quality Score of 7.
Advertiser B has a Max CPC Bid of ₹15 and Quality Score of 9.
Both have identical ad extension impact.
**Determine:** Who wins and at what Actual CPC?

---

**Step 1:** Calculate Ad Rank for Advertiser A.

$$Ad\ Rank_A = ₹20 \times 7 = 140$$

**Step 2:** Calculate Ad Rank for Advertiser B.

$$Ad\ Rank_B = ₹15 \times 9 = 135$$

**Step 3:** Determine the winner.

$$Ad\ Rank_A (140) > Ad\ Rank_B (135)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Advertiser A wins the auction.}}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Actual CPC using the second-price formula.

$$Actual\ CPC_A = \frac{Ad\ Rank_B}{QS_A} + ₹0.01$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the values.

$$Actual\ CPC_A = \frac{135}{7} + ₹0.01$$

**Step 6:** Perform the division.

$$Actual\ CPC_A = ₹19.2857 + ₹0.01$$

$$\boxed{Actual\ CPC_A = ₹19.30\ (approximately)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Advertiser A bid ₹20 but pays only ₹19.30. Advertiser B, despite a higher Quality Score, lost the auction — proving that **Quality Score alone cannot win an auction**, but it can significantly **reduce CPC**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Selection Decision Table

| Campaign Objective | Recommended Bidding Strategy | Key KPI to Monitor |
|--------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Brand Awareness (Top Funnel) | Target Impression Share / vCPM | Impression Share % |
| Website Traffic (Mid Funnel) | Maximize Clicks / Manual CPC | Clicks, CTR |
| Lead Generation (Mid-Bottom) | Target CPA / Maximize Conversions | CPA, Conversion Rate |
| E-commerce Sales (Bottom Funnel) | Target ROAS / Maximize Conv. Value | ROAS, Revenue |
| Video Engagement | CPV (Cost Per View) | View Rate, CPV |
| Competitive Conquesting | Target Impression Share (Top %) | Auction Insights |
| New Campaign (No Historical Data) | Manual CPC / Enhanced CPC | CPC, CTR |
| Remarketing (High-Intent Users) | Target ROAS / Target CPA | ROAS, Repeat Purchases |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
