<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define bidding strategy in digital marketing. List any four automated bidding strategies available in Google Ads.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A bidding strategy in digital marketing is a rule-based framework that determines **how much an advertiser is willing to pay for a specific user action** (click, impression, view, or conversion) in a real-time ad auction.

Four automated bidding strategies are:

- **(i) Maximize Conversions:** The platform automatically sets bids to obtain the maximum number of conversions within the daily budget.
- **(ii) Target CPA:** The advertiser sets a target cost-per-acquisition, and the algorithm bids to achieve conversions at that target cost.
- **(iii) Target ROAS:** The advertiser specifies a target return on ad spend (e.g., 400%), and the algorithm prioritizes high-revenue auctions.
- **(iv) Target Impression Share:** The advertiser specifies the desired share of impressions (e.g., 80%), and the algorithm bids aggressively to achieve that visibility.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Four strategies (0.5 each = 2 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the formula for Ad Rank. How does the second-price auction mechanism benefit advertisers?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Ad Rank formula is:

$$Ad\ Rank = Bid\ Amount \times Quality\ Score \times Expected\ Impact\ of\ Ad\ Extensions$$

The **second-price auction** benefits advertisers in the following ways:

- **(i) Encourages Truthful Bidding:** Advertisers can set their true maximum willingness to pay without fear of overpaying.
- **(ii) Cost Efficiency:** Winners pay only the minimum amount needed to beat the competitor below them, not their full bid.
- **(iii) Promotes Quality:** Since Ad Rank depends on Quality Score, advertisers with relevant ads pay less even with lower bids, rewarding ad quality.
- **(iv) Reduced Auction Anxiety:** Advertisers do not need to constantly micro-adjust bids; the system handles optimal pricing.

**Marks Distribution:** Formula (1 mark) + Three benefit points (0.67 each ≈ 2 marks) = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Compare and contrast Manual CPC bidding with Automated (Smart) bidding. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Manual CPC | Automated Smart Bidding |
|-----------|------------|-------------------------|
| **Control** | Full advertiser control | Algorithm-driven |
| **Time Investment** | High — daily monitoring needed | Low — set and optimize |
| **Data Requirement** | Works without historical data | Requires conversion data (30+ conversions/month) |
| **Optimization Speed** | Slow, manual | Fast, ML-driven |
| **Real-Time Adjustments** | No | Yes — uses device, time, audience signals |
| **Cost Efficiency** | Depends on advertiser skill | Generally higher due to ML |

**Advantages of Manual CPC:**
- Maximum control over keyword-level bids
- Ideal for learning phase and small budgets
- Transparent — every rupee is traceable
- Useful for A/B testing bid levels

**Disadvantages of Manual CPC:**
- Time-consuming and labor-intensive
- Cannot process millions of real-time auction signals
- Prone to human error and emotional bidding
- Misses optimization opportunities outside business hours

**Advantages of Automated Bidding:**
- Processes billions of data signals in real-time
- Optimizes for the exact goal (CPA, ROAS, conversions)
- Frees up marketer time for strategy and creative work
- Continuously learns and improves with more data

**Disadvantages of Automated Bidding:**
- Requires sufficient historical conversion data
- Less transparency in algorithm decision-making
- Can overspend if conversion tracking is inaccurate
- May struggle in highly niche or low-volume campaigns

---

**(b) An e-commerce company has the following campaign data for one month:**
- Ad Spend: ₹80,000
- Revenue Generated: ₹4,80,000
- Impressions: 2,00,000
- Clicks: 6,000
- Conversions: 300

**Calculate: (i) CTR, (ii) CPC, (iii) Conversion Rate, (iv) CPA, (v) ROAS. Comment on which bidding strategy should be used for the next month. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CTR Calculation:**

$$CTR = \frac{Clicks}{Impressions} \times 100 = \frac{6{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 3\%$$

**(ii) CPC Calculation:**

$$CPC = \frac{Ad\ Spend}{Clicks} = \frac{₹80{,}000}{6{,}000} = ₹13.33$$

**(iii) Conversion Rate Calculation:**

$$Conversion\ Rate = \frac{Conversions}{Clicks} \times 100 = \frac{300}{6{,}000} \times 100 = 5\%$$

**(iv) CPA Calculation:**

$$CPA = \frac{Ad\ Spend}{Conversions} = \frac{₹80{,}000}{300} = ₹266.67$$

**(v) ROAS Calculation:**

$$ROAS = \frac{Revenue}{Ad\ Spend} \times 100 = \frac{₹4{,}80{,}000}{₹80{,}000} \times 100 = 600\%$$

**Recommended Bidding Strategy:**

Since the company has a strong ROAS of 600% and 300 conversions in a month (sufficient data for Smart Bidding), the **Target ROAS strategy** should be adopted for the next month. Setting a target ROAS of 500% will allow the algorithm to scale revenue while protecting profitability.

**Marks Distribution:** (i) 1 mark + (ii) 1 mark + (iii) 1 mark + (iv) 1 mark + (v) 1 mark + Recommendation 2 marks = **7 marks**.

---

### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the different types of automated bidding strategies in Google Ads. For each, mention one suitable use case. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Maximize Clicks:**
The algorithm automatically sets bids to drive the maximum number of clicks within the daily budget. It analyzes historical click data to identify high-click-probability auctions.
- *Use Case:* A news website launching a new blog section wants to drive initial traffic.

**2. Maximize Conversions:**
The algorithm bids to maximize the **total number of conversions** without setting a specific CPA target.
- *Use Case:* A new lead-generation campaign with no historical CPA benchmark.

**3. Target CPA (tCPA):**
The advertiser sets a desired CPA (e.g., ₹200), and the algorithm bids to achieve as many conversions as possible at or below ₹200.
- *Use Case:* An EdTech company with a fixed ₹500 budget per enrolled student.

**4. Target ROAS (tROAS):**
The advertiser specifies a target return on ad spend (e.g., 400% = ₹4 revenue per ₹1 spent).
- *Use Case:* An e-commerce store with variable product margins wanting to prioritize high-profit items.

**5. Enhanced CPC (eCPC):**
A hybrid model where the platform can adjust manual bids by up to **30% upward or downward** based on conversion likelihood.
- *Use Case:* Advertisers transitioning from manual to fully automated bidding.

**6. Maximize Conversion Value:**
Optimizes for the **total revenue value** of conversions, not just the count.
- *Use Case:* Retailers with a diverse product catalog where some products are worth ₹5,000 and others ₹500.

**7. Target Impression Share:**
The advertiser specifies the desired impression share percentage (e.g., appear in 90% of eligible auctions).
- *Use Case:* Brand defense campaigns where top-of-page visibility is critical.

**8. Cost Per View (CPV):**
Used in video campaigns where payment occurs only when a user watches 30 seconds or interacts.
- *Use Case:* YouTube brand campaigns aiming for engagement over reach.

---

**(b) With a suitable diagram, explain the Google Ads auction process. How does the second-price auction mechanism work? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Auction Process — Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1 — User Search:** A user types a query in Google or browses a Display Network site.
- **Step 2 — Auction Trigger:** Within milliseconds, Google initiates an ad auction.
- **Step 3 — Eligibility Check:** Ads that violate policies, exceed budgets, or do not match the query are filtered out.
- **Step 4 — Ad Rank Computation:** For each eligible ad, the system calculates:

$$Ad\ Rank = Bid \times Quality\ Score \times Expected\ Impact\ of\ Extensions$$

- **Step 5 — Ranking:** Ads are sorted in descending order of Ad Rank.
- **Step 6 — Display & Payment:** Top ads are shown. Each winner pays only the amount needed to outrank the next competitor.

**Second-Price Auction Explanation:**

In a **second-price (Vickrey) auction**, the winner does not pay their full bid. Instead, they pay:

$$Actual\ CPC = \left(\frac{Ad\ Rank\ of\ Competitor\ Below}{Your\ Quality\ Score}\right) + ₹0.01$$

**Example:**
- Advertiser A bids ₹50, QS = 8 → Ad Rank = 400
- Advertiser B bids ₹60, QS = 6 → Ad Rank = 360
- Advertiser A wins. Actual CPC = (360 / 8) + ₹0.01 = ₹45.01

Even though Advertiser A bid ₹50, they pay only ₹45.01. This incentivizes honest bidding.

**Marks Distribution:** Auction steps (3 marks) + Second-price explanation + formula (2 marks) + Example (2 marks) = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Costing 2–4 Marks Each):**
> - **Confusing CPC with CPA:** CPC = cost per CLICK; CPA = cost per CONVERSION. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> - **Wrong Formula for ROAS:** ROAS uses REVENUE, not PROFIT. Using profit is a common error.
> - **Ignoring Quality Score:** Many students write the Ad Rank formula as just "Bid" — but it includes Quality Score AND Ad Extensions. Missing either component = 1 mark lost.
> - **Confusing Maximize Conversions with Target CPA:** The first has NO target; the second DOES have a target.
> - **Forgetting units:** Always write ₹ or $ or % with the answer. A naked number loses a mark.
> - **No diagram in 14-mark questions:** If the question says "explain with a diagram," a missing diagram costs **2 marks minimum**.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Bidding Strategies**

- Bidding strategy = rules for **how much to pay** for a user action.
- Auction type: **Second-price auction** (Google Ads, Meta Ads).
- **Ad Rank Formula:** Bid × Quality Score × Extension Impact.
- **Actual CPC Formula:** (Competitor's Ad Rank ÷ Your QS) + ₹0.01.
- **Manual Bidding:** Full control, time-consuming, requires manual optimization.
- **Smart Bidding:** ML-driven, requires 30+ conversions/month minimum.
- **8 Automated Strategies to Memorize:** Maximize Clicks, Maximize Conversions, Target CPA, Target ROAS, Enhanced CPC, Maximize Conversion Value, Target Impression Share, CPV.
- **Bid Adjustments** are applied on top of base bids for: Device, Location, Time, Audience, Demographics.
- **5 Core KPIs:** CPC, CPA, ROAS, CTR, Conversion Rate.
- **CPM** = Cost per 1,000 impressions (awareness).
- **CPV** = Cost per view (video).
- **Industry Benchmarks:** CTR 2–5%, Conv. Rate 2–5%, ROAS ≥ 400% for profitability in most e-commerce.
- **Best Strategy for E-commerce:** Target ROAS.
- **Best Strategy for Lead Gen:** Target CPA.
- **Best Strategy for Traffic:** Maximize Clicks.
- **Best Strategy for Brand Defense:** Target Impression Share.
- **Quality Score is rated 1–10** and directly affects CPC.
- **Bid Adjustments use percentage values** (e.g., +20% mobile, -30% off-peak hours).
- **Always end auction analysis with optimization recommendations**, not just calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing and Monitoring Search Campaigns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET833 - DIGITAL MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Designing and Monitoring Search Campaigns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Alignment):**
> A **Search Campaign** is a structured, paid Search Engine Marketing (SEM) initiative in which advertisers bid on specific keywords to display clickable text advertisements within the Search Engine Results Pages (SERPs) of platforms such as Google, Bing, or Yahoo. **Designing** refers to the pre-launch strategic architecture (keyword selection, ad copy, bidding, targeting), while **Monitoring** refers to the post-launch analytical supervision using KPIs like CTR, CPC, CPA, and Quality Score to ensure ROI.

In the context of **Search Engine Marketing (SEM)**, search campaigns form the commercial backbone of Pay-Per-Click (PPC) advertising. Every search campaign is governed by an **Auction-Based System** in which advertisers compete in real-time auctions each time a user enters a search query.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Real-World Analogy — "The Digital Billboard on a Toll Road"**
>
> Imagine a busy highway where every car owner passing through represents an internet user. The highway has multiple **toll booths** (Google Search Results). You, as a shopkeeper, want to display your shop's name on the digital billboard placed right *before* the toll booth so that drivers immediately know where to stop.
>
> - The **toll booth position** = Search Engine Ranking Position (Position 1, 2, 3, etc.).
> - The **money you pay** = Bid Amount / CPC (Cost Per Click).
> - The **billboard text** = Ad Copy (Headline + Description).
> - The **type of drivers you target** = Keyword Targeting.
> - The **billboard's attractiveness and clarity** = Quality Score.
>
> If your billboard is attractive (high CTR), clearly relevant to the driver's needs (Ad Relevance), and points to a well-organized shop (Landing Page Experience), the toll-road operator (Google) will charge you *less* for a prime position. **Designing** is *building the billboard*, while **Monitoring** is *checking daily how many drivers stop, what they buy, and whether the rent you pay is profitable*.

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics in Search Campaigns

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Metrics Memorized by KTU Board Examiners:**
> - **CTR (Click-Through Rate)** — Measures ad attractiveness.
> - **CPC (Cost Per Click)** — Measures cost efficiency of each click.
> - **CPA (Cost Per Acquisition)** — Measures cost to acquire a customer.
> - **Conversion Rate (CVR)** — Measures landing page effectiveness.
> - **ROAS (Return On Ad Spend)** — Measures profitability ratio.
> - **Quality Score (QS)** — Measures ad keyword quality on a scale of **1 to 10**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture of a Search Campaign

A Google Search Campaign follows a strict **hierarchical structure** (often asked in 2-mark and 3-mark questions):

1. **Account Level** — The master billing and administrative container.
2. **Campaign Level** — Defines budget, geographic targeting, bidding strategy, and ad schedule.
3. **Ad Group Level** — Groups related keywords and the ads that target them.
4. **Keywords & Ads Level** — The granular unit where keywords match user queries and trigger ads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Hierarchy?**
> The hierarchy exists because Google requires *relevance at scale*. Grouping tightly-related keywords together with tightly-related ad copy ensures a high **Quality Score**, which directly lowers **CPC**.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Campaign Design

### Pillar 1: Keyword Strategy
Keywords are the *triggers* that connect user intent to your ad. They are categorized by **Match Types**:

| Match Type | Syntax | Trigger Logic | Reach vs Precision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Broad Match** | `digital marketing` | Any query loosely related | Maximum Reach, Lowest Precision |
| **Phrase Match** | `"digital marketing"` | Query contains the phrase | Moderate Reach, Moderate Precision |
| **Exact Match** | `[digital marketing]` | Query is exactly the term (or close variant) | Minimum Reach, Maximum Precision |
| **Negative Match** | `-free` | Excludes queries containing the term | Used to filter irrelevant traffic |

### Pillar 2: Ad Copywriting
Text ads follow the **Expanded Text Ad (ETA)** structure on Google Search:

- **Headlines (H1, H2, H3)** — Up to 30 characters each.
- **Description Lines (D1, D2)** — Up to 90 characters each.
- **Display URL Path** — Optional, 15 characters each.
- **Call-to-Action (CTA)** — Critical conversion trigger.

### Pillar 3: Bidding & Budgeting
Advertisers set a **daily budget** and a **bidding strategy**:

| Bidding Strategy | Optimization Goal | KTU Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Manual CPC** | Advertiser sets max CPC per click | Best for tight budgets |
| **Enhanced CPC** | Google adjusts manual bids based on CVR likelihood | Mid-level optimization |
| **Target CPA** | Google bids to achieve target acquisition cost | Lead generation |
| **Target ROAS** | Google bids to achieve target return ratio | E-commerce |
| **Maximize Clicks** | Spend full budget to get as many clicks as possible | Traffic campaigns |
| **Maximize Conversions** | Spend budget to get max conversions within budget | Conversion campaigns |

## 2.3 The Quality Score Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Quality Score (QS)** is Google's 1-to-10 rating of the *user experience* your ad and landing page provide. A high QS lowers CPC and improves Ad Rank.

The **three pillars of Quality Score** (often tested):

1. **Expected Click-Through Rate (CTR)** — Based on past performance of similar ads.
2. **Ad Relevance** — How closely your ad copy matches the user's search intent.
3. **Landing Page Experience** — How useful, relevant, and fast your post-click page is.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all the high-yield formulas essential for KTU Board Examination problem-solving:

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Strategic Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CTR** | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Ad attractiveness |
| **CPC** | $\text{CPC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Clicks}}$ | Currency (₹/$) | Cost per visitor |
| **Conversion Rate** | $\text{CVR} = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Clicks}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Landing page efficacy |
| **CPA** | $\text{CPA} = \dfrac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{Conversions}}$ | Currency (₹/$) | Cost per customer |
| **ROAS** | $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Profitability ratio |
| **Ad Rank** | $\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Bid Amount} \times \text{Quality Score}$ | Score Index | Determines SERP position |
| **Total Budget** | $\text{Budget} = \text{Daily Budget} \times 30$ | Currency | Monthly planning |
| **Effective CPC** | $\text{Effective CPC} = \dfrac{\text{Competitor Ad Rank}}{\text{Your QS}} + 0.01$ | Currency | Actual amount paid in auction |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering / Industry Relevance:**
> - **E-Commerce**: Google Shopping & Search Ads are primary revenue drivers for Flipkart, Amazon, Myntra.
> - **B2B SaaS**: LinkedIn + Google Search campaigns generate qualified enterprise leads.
> - **EdTech**: Platforms like Coursera, Unacademy use search campaigns to capture high-intent learners.
> - **Healthcare**: Hospitals bid on geo-targeted keywords like "cardiology hospital near me".

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Expected CPC Using Ad Rank Auction

The Google Ads auction is a **Second-Price Auction Variant**. The actual CPC paid by the winner is determined by the Ad Rank of the competitor just below them.

### Given Data:
- Your **Bid Amount** = ₹50
- Your **Quality Score** = 8
- Competitor's **Bid Amount** = ₹40
- Competitor's **Quality Score** = 10

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Calculate your Ad Rank.
$$\text{Ad Rank}_{\text{You}} = \text{Bid} \times \text{QS} = 50 \times 8 = 400$$

**Step 2:** Calculate competitor's Ad Rank (the rank just below yours).
$$\text{Ad Rank}_{\text{Competitor}} = 40 \times 10 = 400$$

**Step 3:** Apply the second-price formula. The minimum bid you need to beat the competitor is derived by equating their Ad Ranks.
$$\text{Min Bid to Match Competitor} = \dfrac{\text{Ad Rank}_{\text{Competitor}}}{\text{Your QS}} + 0.01$$
$$\text{Min Bid} = \dfrac{400}{8} + 0.01 = 50.01$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.
> [!NOTE]
> Even though you bid ₹50 and competitor bid ₹40, because your QS (8) is lower than theirs (10), you must bid at least ₹50.01 to outrank them. This demonstrates that **Quality Score directly reduces CPC**.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Campaign Profitability Using ROAS

A KTU-favorite numerical problem involves computing whether a campaign is profitable.

### Given Data:
- Total Ad Spend = ₹1,00,000
- Total Clicks = 5,000
- Total Conversions = 250
- Average Order Value = ₹800

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Calculate the **Click-Through Rate** (CTR). However, impressions are not given. CTR is therefore not directly derivable from this dataset; we proceed to CPC.

**Step 2:** Calculate the **Cost Per Click (CPC)**.
$$\text{CPC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Clicks}} = \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = ₹20 \text{ per click}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the **Conversion Rate (CVR)**.
$$\text{CVR} = \dfrac{250}{5{,}000} \times 100 = 5\%$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the **Cost Per Acquisition (CPA)**.
$$\text{CPA} = \dfrac{1{,}00{,}000}{250} = ₹400 \text{ per conversion}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the **Total Revenue Generated**.
$$\text{Revenue} = \text{Conversions} \times \text{Order Value} = 250 \times 800 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the **Return On Ad Spend (ROAS)**.
$$\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 200\%$$

**Step 7:** Compute **Net Profit**.
$$\text{Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Ad Spend} = 2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}00{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Verdict:** A ROAS of **200%** means every ₹1 spent returns ₹2 in revenue, yielding a net positive profit of ₹1,00,000. The campaign is **profitable**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Keyword Match Type Selection

This matrix guides campaign designers on when to deploy each match type (frequently asked in 14-mark design questions):

| Campaign Phase | Recommended Match Type | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initial Launch (Discovery)** | Broad Match | Discover new keyword variations and search behavior. |
| **Optimization (Refinement)** | Phrase Match | Balance volume with relevance after data accumulation. |
| **Mature / High-Performance** | Exact Match | Maximize precision and ROAS on proven keywords. |
| **Always (Across Phases)** | Negative Keywords | Block irrelevant queries that waste budget. |

## 3.4 The A/B Testing Derivation (Ad Copy Optimization)

To scientifically improve an ad campaign, an A/B test is conducted:

### Given Data:
- **Variant A (Control):** 1,000 impressions, 50 clicks, 5 conversions.
- **Variant B (Test):** 1,000 impressions, 70 clicks, 8 conversions.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Compute CTR for both variants.
$$\text{CTR}_A = \dfrac{50}{1000} \times 100 = 5\% \qquad \text{CTR}_B = \dfrac{70}{1000} \times 100 = 7\%$$

**Step 2:** Compute Conversion Rate for both variants.
$$\text{CVR}_A = \dfrac{5}{50} \times 100 = 10\% \qquad \text{CVR}_B = \dfrac{8}{70} \times 100 \approx 11.43\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute CPA for both variants (assume ₹20 CPC).
$$\text{CPA}_A = \dfrac{1000 \times 0.20}{5} = ₹40 \qquad \text{CPA}_B = \dfrac{1400 \times 0.20}{8} = ₹35$$

**Step 4:** Final Decision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Variant B wins on **all three dimensions** (higher CTR, higher CVR, lower CPA). Variant B's ad copy should be **scaled**, while Variant A is paused.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Search Campaign

The following Mermaid block renders the **Campaign → Ad Group → Keyword → Ad** hierarchy that governs every search advertising effort:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Google Ads Account] --> B1[Campaign 1 Brand Keywords]
    A1 --> B2[Campaign 2 Generic Keywords]
    B1 --> C1[Ad Group A Headphones]
    B1 --> C2[Ad Group B Smartphones]
    B2 --> C3[Ad Group C Buy Electronics Online]
    B2 --> C4[Ad Group D Best Deals Kerala]
    C1 --> D1[Keyword wireless headphones]
    C1 --> D2[Keyword bluetooth earphones]
    C1 --> E1[Text Ad Headline Free Shipping]
    C2 --> D3[Keyword android smartphone]
    C2 --> E2[Text Ad Headline EMI Available]
    C3 --> D4[Keyword online electronics store]
    C3 --> E3[Text Ad Headline Upto 50 Percent Off]
    C4 --> D5[Keyword cheap electronics Kerala]
    C4 --> E4[Text Ad Headline Fast Delivery]
```

## 4.2 Search Campaign Design-to-Monitoring Workflow

This flowchart captures the **end-to-end campaign lifecycle** from research to optimization:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Define Business Goal] --> S2[Step 2 Keyword Research]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Campaign Structuring]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Ad Copywriting]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Bid and Budget Allocation]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Launch Campaign]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Monitor KPIs Daily]
    S7 --> S8{Quality Score Above 7?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Scale and Expand Keywords]
    S8 -- No --> S10[Optimize Ad Copy and Landing Page]
    S10 --> S7
    S9 --> S11[Weekly Performance Review]
    S11 --> S12[Monthly ROI Analysis and Reporting]
```

## 4.3 Monitoring Dashboard Architecture

This block diagram represents the **Google Ads Monitoring Interface**, mapping the input metrics to output decisions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inp1[Impressions Data] --> Met1[CTR Calculation]
    Inp2[Clicks Data] --> Met1
    Met1 --> Met2[CPC Calculation]
    Inp3[Cost Data] --> Met2
    Inp2 --> Met3[Conversion Rate Calculation]
    Inp4[Conversions Data] --> Met3
    Met2 --> Met4[CPA Calculation]
    Inp3 --> Met4
    Met4 --> Met5[ROAS Calculation]
    Inp5[Revenue Data] --> Met5
    Met5 --> Dec{ROAS Above Target?}
    Dec -- Yes --> Out1[Increase Budget and Scale]
    Dec -- No --> Out2[Pause Underperforming Keywords]
    Out2 --> Dec
```

## 4.4 Ad Rank Auction Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since the auction mechanism is complex, the following **functional block diagram** illustrates how Google determines ad positioning:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Search Query] --> M[Keyword Match Evaluation]
    M --> E[Eligibility Check on Policy and Budget]
    E --> A1[Advertiser A Bid 40 with QS 8]
    E --> A2[Advertiser B Bid 30 with QS 10]
    E --> A3[Advertiser C Bid 50 with QS 5]
    A1 --> R1[Ad Rank 320]
    A2 --> R2[Ad Rank 300]
    A3 --> R3[Ad Rank 250]
    R1 --> P1[Position 1 Top of Page]
    R2 --> P2[Position 2]
    R3 --> P3[Position 3]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Auction:** Note how Advertiser B beats Advertiser A despite a *lower bid*, purely because of a superior Quality Score. This is the central pedagogical insight of search campaign bidding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEPET833]`
**Define Quality Score. List its three core components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Score (QS)** is Google's 1-to-10 diagnostic rating of the overall quality of an advertisement, measured by how relevant the ad copy, keyword, and landing page are to the person seeing the ad.
>
> The **three core components** are:
> 1. **Expected Click-Through Rate (CTR):** Likelihood that the ad receives clicks based on past performance.
> 2. **Ad Relevance:** Degree to which the ad copy matches the intent of the user's search query.
> 3. **Landing Page Experience:** Utility, relevance, transparency, and ease-of-navigation of the post-click destination page.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEPET833]`
**Explain the difference between Broad Match, Phrase Match, and Exact Match keywords with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> - **Broad Match** shows the ad for any search query loosely related to the keyword. Example: Keyword `shoes` matches queries like "buy sneakers online", "red shoes for women", etc.
> - **Phrase Match** shows the ad only when the query contains the keyword phrase in exact order. Example: `"red shoes"` matches "buy red shoes online" but not "shoes that are red".
> - **Exact Match** shows the ad only when the query is exactly the keyword or a close variant. Example: `[red shoes]` matches "red shoes" and "buy red shoes" only when intent is identical.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEPET833]` — 14 Marks

**"Design a complete Google Search Campaign for a new fitness app targeting users in Kerala. Explain the campaign structure, keyword strategy, ad copy, bidding approach, and monitoring KPIs."**

#### Part (a) — Campaign Structure, Keyword Strategy, and Ad Copy (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**1. Campaign Structure Design:**
- One master **Account** for the fitness brand.
- **Campaign 1:** Brand Campaign (Keywords like app name).
- **Campaign 2:** Generic Campaign (Keywords like "fitness app Kerala").
- **Campaign 3:** Competitor Campaign (Keywords like rival app names).
- Within each campaign, **Ad Groups** are created by intent: "Weight Loss", "Yoga", "Home Workout".

**2. Keyword Strategy:**
- Use **Phrase Match** for initial launch: `"fitness app Kerala"`, `"home workout app"`.
- Use **Exact Match** for high-intent searches: `[best fitness app free]`.
- Use **Broad Match** only for the discovery phase with strict budget caps.
- Deploy a strong **Negative Keyword list**: `-jobs`, `-review`, `-crack`, `-download free mod`.

**3. Ad Copy Design (Expanded Text Ad):**
- **Headline 1:** Kerala Fitness App (30 chars)
- **Headline 2:** Start Your Home Workout (30 chars)
- **Headline 3:** Free 7 Day Trial (30 chars)
- **Description 1:** Get personalized workout plans designed by certified Kerala trainers. Begin your journey today.
- **Description 2:** Track calories, monitor progress, and join 10,000+ users transforming their health.

**Ad Extensions** added: Sitelinks (Download Now, Pricing, Trainers), Callout Extensions (24/7 Support, No Hidden Fees), Structured Snippets (Features: Calorie Tracker, Video Workouts, Yoga Plans).

#### Part (b) — Bidding Approach and Monitoring KPIs (7 Marks)

**1. Bidding Approach:**
- **Daily Budget:** ₹2,000 during launch, scaling to ₹10,000 in month 2.
- **Initial Bidding Strategy:** **Maximize Clicks** with a max CPC cap of ₹30 to gather traffic data.
- **Mature Phase Bidding:** Switch to **Target CPA** at ₹150 once 30+ conversions are recorded.
- **E-commerce Variant:** If selling subscriptions, use **Target ROAS** at 300%.

**2. Monitoring KPIs (Daily, Weekly, Monthly Cadence):**
- **Daily:** Check spend pacing, CTR anomalies, and disapproved ads.
- **Weekly:** Review Search Terms Report, add negative keywords, optimize ad copy via A/B testing.
- **Monthly:** Evaluate ROAS, CPA, and Conversion Rate by Ad Group. Pause keywords with QS below 5.

**3. Optimization Loops:**
- If CTR < 2%, rewrite ad copy with stronger CTAs.
- If CVR < 3%, redesign the landing page for mobile speed.
- If QS < 5, segregate the keyword into its own tightly-themed Ad Group.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEPET833]` — 14 Marks

**"Explain the key metrics used to monitor a search campaign. A campaign generated 80,000 impressions, 2,000 clicks, 120 conversions, and ₹40,000 ad spend with a revenue of ₹1,20,000. Calculate CTR, CPC, CVR, CPA, and ROAS. Comment on profitability."**

#### Part (a) — Key Monitoring Metrics Explanation (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**1. Impressions** — Number of times the ad was shown. Indicates *reach* and *visibility*.

**2. Click-Through Rate (CTR)** — Measures ad attractiveness. Industry benchmark for Search Ads is **3–5%**.

**3. Cost Per Click (CPC)** — Average amount paid per click. Lower CPC = higher efficiency.

**4. Conversion Rate (CVR)** — Percentage of clicks that complete the desired action (purchase, sign-up).

**5. Cost Per Acquisition (CPA)** — Total cost to acquire one converting customer.

**6. Return On Ad Spend (ROAS)** — Revenue earned per rupee spent. Target benchmark = **4:1 (400%)** for e-commerce.

**7. Quality Score (QS)** — Internal Google score affecting CPC and ad position.

**8. Search Terms Report** — Actual queries that triggered the ad. Source of negative keywords.

**9. Auction Insights** — Competitive visibility metric showing competitor performance.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation and Profitability Comment (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate CTR**
$$\text{CTR} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{80{,}000} \times 100 = 2.5\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate CPC**
$$\text{CPC} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{2{,}000} = ₹20 \text{ per click}$$

**Step 3: Calculate CVR**
$$\text{CVR} = \dfrac{120}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 6\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate CPA**
$$\text{CPA} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{120} = ₹333.33 \text{ per conversion}$$

**Step 5: Calculate ROAS**
$$\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{40{,}000} \times 100 = 300\%$$

**Step 6: Profitability Verdict**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Verdict:** A ROAS of **300%** means every ₹1 spent returns ₹3 in revenue. Net profit = ₹1,20,000 − ₹40,000 = **₹80,000**. The campaign is **profitable**. However, CTR (2.5%) is below the ideal 3% benchmark, indicating ad copy optimization opportunity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Search Campaign Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing CTR with Conversion Rate.** CTR measures *click-through*, CVR measures *goal completion*. Examiners strictly deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Formulas.** Always write "₹ per click" or "%" — vague numerical answers lose half-marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Quality Score in Bidding Questions.** Any auction-related question MUST mention QS; examiners award 2 marks for explicitly stating its impact.
> 4. **Using "Customer" instead of "Conversion".** "Conversion" is the correct SEM terminology for any completed goal, including sign-ups, not just purchases.
> 5. **Not Providing Negative Keywords in Design Questions.** A design answer without a negative keyword list is considered incomplete (loses 1 mark).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep:**
> - A Search Campaign is a **paid, auction-based PPC** initiative running on SERPs.
> - The hierarchy is **Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Keywords/Ads**.
> - **Four Match Types:** Broad, Phrase, Exact, Negative. Negative keywords *filter* irrelevant traffic.
> - **Quality Score** has three pillars: **Expected CTR, Ad Relevance, Landing Page Experience**.
> - The auction formula is: **Ad Rank = Bid Amount × Quality Score**.
> - **Higher QS = Lower CPC and Better Position** — the most-tested insight.
> - **CTR Formula:** $\text{Clicks} / \text{Impressions} \times 100$.
> - **CPC Formula:** $\text{Total Cost} / \text{Total Clicks}$.
> - **ROAS Formula:** $\text{Revenue} / \text{Ad Spend} \times 100$.
> - **Bidding Strategies:** Manual CPC, Enhanced CPC, Target CPA, Target ROAS, Maximize Clicks, Maximize Conversions.
> - **Monitoring Cadence:** Daily (spend/disapproval), Weekly (search terms/A/B testing), Monthly (ROAS scaling).
> - **Ad Extensions** like Sitelinks, Callouts, and Structured Snippets boost CTR and QS.
> - **Landing Page Experience** affects QS — speed, mobile-friendliness, and relevance matter.
> - Always include **Negative Keywords** in any campaign design question to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Display Campaigns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET833 - DIGITAL MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Display Campaigns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Display Campaign** is a paid, advertiser-initiated digital advertising strategy that delivers visually engaging creatives (image, banner, rich media, video, or native units) across a global network of third-party publisher websites, mobile applications, and video partner properties — collectively termed the **Google Display Network (GDN)** or any comparable programmatic ad-exchange ecosystem (such as Meta Audience Network, Microsoft Audience Network, or programmatic DSPs like DV360, The Trade Desk, and Amazon DSP).

In KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based terminology, a Display Campaign is defined as:

> A non-search, intent-interruption model of digital advertising that uses visual creatives, audience signals, contextual placements, and automated bidding to influence **passive audiences** who are *not actively searching* for the advertised product, but whose demographic, psychographic, behavioural, or contextual profile makes them probabilistically likely to convert.

Display Campaigns operate on the **pay-per-impression (PPM / CPM)** model by default but can be transacted via **CPC (cost-per-click)**, **CPA (cost-per-acquisition)**, **vCPM (viewable CPM)**, or **tCPA (target CPA)** bidding strategies, depending on the campaign objective configured in the advertiser's ad-server (Google Ads, Meta Ads Manager, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Display Campaigns sit within the broader *Paid Media* pillar of the digital marketing mix (Module 3), complementing Search Engine Marketing (SEM), Social Media Advertising, and Email Marketing. The core examination focus is on **campaign architecture, targeting taxonomy, bidding mechanics, and performance KPIs**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

Imagine you own a popular clothing brand and you have two distinct options to advertise:

### Option A — The Street Billboard (Search Ads equivalent)
A customer is actively walking down MG Road, Kochi, **specifically searching** for a "cotton kurta for Onam." You put your shop sign right in their path. They were already looking — your ad simply **responds** to their intent. This is **Search Advertising**.

### Option B — The Newspaper Insert / Mall Poster (Display Ads equivalent)
A customer is sitting at a Cochin International Airport waiting lounge, casually flipping through a *Vanitha* magazine, or scrolling through *Manorama Online*. They are **not actively searching** for clothing. However, because they are a 28-year-old Malayali female flying to Bangalore for a tech interview, you place a beautifully designed, culturally resonant Onam-collection ad in their reading flow. They pause, click, browse, and buy — **driven by the creative's relevance, not by an active query**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Analogy Distilled:**
> - **Search Ads** = Reactive. Responds to an existing demand signal (the typed query).
> - **Display Ads** = Proactive. **Manufactures or anticipates** demand by inserting visually persuasive messaging into an audience's passive consumption stream.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary for the KTU Board Examiner

The following **standard metrics and acronyms** must be memorised verbatim. Each is a high-yield term that frequently appears as a 2-mark definitional question.

| Term | Full Form | Definition (Examiner-Ready) |
|------|-----------|----------------------------|
| **CPM** | Cost Per Mille | Cost an advertiser pays per **1,000 impressions** of the ad unit. |
| **CPC** | Cost Per Click | Cost incurred only when a user actually **clicks** the creative. |
| **CTR** | Click-Through Rate | The percentage of impressions that **result in a click**. |
| **vCPM** | Viewable Cost Per Mille | Cost per 1,000 **measurable viewable** impressions (≥ 50% pixels on-screen for 1 sec for display, 2 sec for video). |
| **CPA / tCPA** | Cost Per Acquisition / Target CPA | Cost per completed **conversion event** (sale, lead, sign-up). |
| **GDN** | Google Display Network | Google's collective pool of 2M+ partner websites, apps, and video properties where Display ads can serve. |
| **DSP** | Demand-Side Platform | Programmatic software used by advertisers to buy ad inventory in real-time. |
| **SSP** | Supply-Side Platform | Publisher-side software that sells inventory to multiple ad exchanges. |
| **Ad Exchange** | — | A digital marketplace facilitating **real-time bidding (RTB)** between DSPs and SSPs. |
| **Remarketing** | — | Re-engaging users who previously visited your site/app but did not convert. |
| **Ad Rank** | — | A composite score determining ad placement, derived from Bid × Quality × Format Impact. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold-Mandated Constants:** **CPM (Cost Per Mille)** and **GDN (Google Display Network)** are the two most examination-frequent acronyms in Module 3 Display Campaign questions. The denominator in CPM is **always 1,000** impressions — this is the Latin root *"mille"*.

---

## 1.4 Why Display Campaigns Exist — The Business Problem They Solve

Search ads have an inherent scalability ceiling — they can only reach users who *type* a relevant query. Display solves three problems:

1. **Awareness Generation for New Categories:** A startup launching an *AI-powered Ayurvedic wellness app* cannot rely on users searching for it (no one knows it exists). Display places the brand directly into the user's environment.
2. **Remarketing / Cart Recovery:** E-commerce studies (Google internal benchmarks) show **~70% of online shopping carts are abandoned**. Display remarketing tags these users and re-serves tailored creatives ("Still thinking about that kurta? 10% off today only").
3. **Top-of-Funnel Demand Capture:** Display reaches audiences at the *awareness* and *consideration* stages of the marketing funnel, where Search only operates at the *decision* stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Marketing Funnel Mapping (KTU 2024 PLO Mapping):**
> - **TOFU (Top of Funnel — Awareness)** → Display & Video Campaigns
> - **MOFU (Middle of Funnel — Consideration)** → Remarketing Display, Social
> - **BOFU (Bottom of Funnel — Decision)** → Search, Shopping, Performance Max
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Structure of a Display Campaign

Every Display Campaign — whether run on Google Ads, Meta, or programmatic DSPs — is composed of a **three-tier hierarchical structure**:

```
LEVEL 1:  ACCOUNT          → The advertiser's master login container
           │
LEVEL 2:  CAMPAIGN         → Houses budget, objective, geo-targeting, schedule
           │
LEVEL 3:  AD GROUP          → Contains specific targeting criteria + creatives
           │
LEVEL 4:  AD / CREATIVE     → The actual banner/video/image served to users
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern:** Board questions often test the *campaign hierarchy*. Memorise the order: **Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Ad**. A common 3-mark question is *"Explain the structure of a Google Ads Display Campaign with a neat diagram."*

### 2.1.1 Level 1 — Campaign
This is the **strategic layer**. Here, the advertiser defines:
- **Objective** (Brand Awareness, Consideration, Conversions, Traffic, Lead Gen, App Promotion).
- **Budget** (Daily or campaign-total, with optional shared budget across campaigns).
- **Bid Strategy** (Manual CPC, Target CPA, Target ROAS, Maximize Conversions, Enhanced CPC, Viewable CPM).
- **Location & Language Targeting.**
- **Campaign Schedule** (start/end dates, ad-scheduling by hour/day).
- **Device Bid Modifiers** (mobile, desktop, tablet adjustments).

### 2.1.2 Level 2 — Ad Group
This is the **targeting layer**. An ad group bundles together:
- **Targeting methods** (audience, contextual, placement, topic, demographic).
- **Creative assets** (images, headlines, descriptions, logos, videos).
- **Frequency caps** (max impressions per user per day/week).
- **Audience exclusions** (negative audience lists).

### 2.1.3 Level 3 — Ad / Creative
The **execution layer**. Multiple ad variants (responsive display ads, uploaded image ads, HTML5 rich media) are grouped for Google's machine learning to **rotate, test, and optimise** based on performance signals.

---

## 2.2 Targeting Taxonomy — The Five Core Methods

Display Campaigns offer five primary targeting methods. The KTU examiner frequently asks students to **classify a given targeting scenario** into the correct method.

| # | Targeting Method | Definition | Real-World Use Case |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Audience Targeting** | Targets users based on who they are (interests, behaviours, in-market signals, life events). | Targeting "in-market for wedding apparel" for bridal collection. |
| 2 | **Contextual Targeting** | Targets pages based on thematic content (keywords, topics, page categories). | Showing a laptop ad on a "best laptops under ₹50,000" article. |
| 3 | **Placement Targeting** | Manually selects specific websites, YouTube channels, apps, or video URLs. | Selecting ManoramaOnline.com as a hand-picked inventory partner. |
| 4 | **Demographic Targeting** | Filters by age, gender, household income, parental status. | Showing luxury watches only to males aged 30–55, top 10% income. |
| 5 | **Remarketing Targeting** | Re-engages past site visitors using cookie-based audience lists. | Cart-abandoners shown a 15% discount creative within 7 days. |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Targeting Matrix Insight:** Modern Display Campaigns combine **multiple targeting methods in a single ad group** using the *AND* logic. For example: `(Audience: in-market for running shoes) AND (Demographic: age 25–40) AND (Contextual: keywords "marathon training")`. This is called **layered targeting**.

---

## 2.3 Ad Formats — Creative Taxonomy

The Google Display Network supports the following ad formats. Each has distinct engineering, cost, and engagement implications.

### 2.3.1 Static Image Ads
- **IAB Standard Sizes:** 300×250 (Medium Rectangle), 728×90 (Leaderboard), 160×600 (Skyscraper), 320×50 (Mobile Banner).
- File format: JPG, PNG, GIF (static).
- Maximum file size: **150 KB** for Google Ads.

### 2.3.2 Animated GIF / HTML5 Ads
- Animation duration: ≤ 30 seconds; loop limit: 3 loops.
- HTML5 enables clickable, interactive, and responsive creative units.

### 2.3.3 Responsive Display Ads (RDA)
- Advertiser uploads up to **15 images**, 5 headlines, 5 descriptions, and 1 logo.
- Google's ML automatically generates combinations and serves the **best-performing creative** per impression context (device, audience, placement).
- This is the **default and recommended** format since Google Ads 2018 update.

### 2.3.4 Video Ads
- Skippable in-stream (TrueView), non-skippable in-stream (15 sec), bumper (6 sec), out-stream.
- Bid by **CPV (cost-per-view)** for TrueView or **CPM** for bumper/non-skippable.

### 2.3.5 Rich Media / Interactive Ads
- Expandable units, interstitials, parallax, and playable ads (predominantly for in-app).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The KTU examiner will deduct marks if a student confuses **Responsive Display Ads (RDA)** with **Responsive Search Ads (RSA)**. They are **different products**. RDA = visual display units. RSA = text-based search units.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Display Campaign Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **the most examination-frequent calculations** in Module 3. The 14-mark derivations frequently require chaining 2–3 of these into a single numerical problem.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Units | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---------|------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Cost Per Mille (CPM)** | $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 1000$ | ₹ per 1000 impressions | Brand awareness budget allocation |
| 2 | **Cost Per Click (CPC)** | $\text{CPC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Clicks}}$ | ₹ per click | Traffic generation campaigns |
| 3 | **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Creative performance benchmarking |
| 4 | **Cost Per Acquisition (CPA)** | $\text{CPA} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Conversions}}$ | ₹ per conversion | E-commerce ROAS analysis |
| 5 | **View-Through Rate (VTR)** | $\text{VTR} = \dfrac{\text{Views}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Video display ad effectiveness |
| 6 | **Frequency** | $\text{Frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Impressions}}{\text{Unique Reach}}$ | Times per user | Ad fatigue analysis |
| 7 | **Effective Cost Per Mille (eCPM)** | $\text{eCPM} = \text{CPC} \times \text{CTR} \times 1000$ | ₹ per 1000 impressions | Cross-bid-strategy comparison |
| 8 | **Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)** | $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Total Ad Cost}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Conversion campaign profitability |
| 9 | **Viewable CPM (vCPM)** | $\text{vCPM} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Viewable Impressions}} \times 1000$ | ₹ per 1000 viewable impressions | Brand-safe viewability-based buys |
| 10 | **Ad Rank (Display)** | $\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Bid} \times \text{Quality Score} \times \text{Format Impact}$ | Dimensionless score | Auction winner determination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-Symbol Substitution Rule:** In any LaTeX expression requiring absolute value, write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\lvert\text{Numerator}\rvert$ — **never** use the vertical pipe `|` character inside markdown table cells, as it breaks table syntax.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

Display Campaign metrics are not just marketing KPIs — they drive **algorithmic decision-making in real-time bidding (RTB) engines** that execute over **120 million ad auctions per second globally** (Google internal data). The quality score components and bid values feed **bid-shading algorithms**, **pacing engines** (which smooth budget delivery across the day), and **frequency-capping models** that prevent ad fatigue. In B.Tech project contexts, students building advertising analytics dashboards (e.g., a final-year mini-project using the Google Ads API) directly consume these formulas to compute campaign KPIs from raw impression and click logs.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — From Raw Logs to CTR

**Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]:**
A Display Campaign serves **2,40,000 impressions** during a 7-day flight. The advertiser records **1,800 clicks** and **75 conversions**. The total ad spend was **₹36,000**. Compute the CTR, CPC, CPA, and CPM. Given the campaign goal was "Lead Generation" with an average lead value of ₹800, determine whether the campaign was profitable.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1: Tabulate the Given Data**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impressions} &= 2{,}40{,}000 \\
\text{Clicks} &= 1{,}800 \\
\text{Conversions} &= 75 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{₹}36{,}000 \\
\text{Lead Value (Revenue per Conversion)} &= \text{₹}800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the CTR (Click-Through Rate)**

Substituting into the CTR formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CTR} &= \frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}800}{2{,}40{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}800}{2{,}400} \\
&= 0.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> The CTR of **0.75%** falls within the *Google Display Network industry benchmark range of 0.5%–1%*. A creative optimisation is not urgent.

**Step 3: Compute the CPC (Cost Per Click)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPC} &= \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Clicks}} \\
&= \frac{36{,}000}{1{,}800} \\
&= \text{₹}20 \text{ per click}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the CPA (Cost Per Acquisition)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPA} &= \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Conversions}} \\
&= \frac{36{,}000}{75} \\
&= \text{₹}480 \text{ per conversion}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the CPM (Cost Per Mille)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPM} &= \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{36{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} \times 1000 \\
&= 0.15 \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹}150 \text{ per 1,000 impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Total Revenue and ROAS**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue} &= \text{Conversions} \times \text{Lead Value} \\
&= 75 \times 800 \\
&= \text{₹}60{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROAS} &= \frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{60{,}000}{36{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 1.667 \times 100 \\
&= 166.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Profitability Verdict**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= \text{Revenue} - \text{Cost} \\
&= 60{,}000 - 36{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}24{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The campaign is **profitable** with a net gain of ₹24,000 over 7 days and a ROAS of **166.67%** (meaning every ₹1 spent returns ₹1.67 in revenue). CPA (₹480) is **40% lower** than lead value (₹800), confirming a positive unit economics structure.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Effective CPM (eCPM) Bridge Across Bid Strategies

**Problem Statement:**
A Display Campaign uses **CPC bidding at ₹15** and achieves a **CTR of 0.6%**. The same advertiser is being pitched a **CPM-based deal at ₹80** by a publisher. Determine whether the CPC model is more cost-efficient than the CPM deal, using the **eCPM bridge formula**.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1: Recall the eCPM Bridge Formula**

The Effective CPM converts a CPC-bid performance into an equivalent CPM value, allowing apples-to-apples comparison:

$$
\text{eCPM} = \text{CPC} \times \text{CTR} \times 1000
$$

**Step 2: Plug in the Given Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{eCPM}_{\text{CPC deal}} &= 15 \times 0.006 \times 1000 \\
&= 0.09 \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹}90 \text{ per 1,000 impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: $0.6\% = 0.006$ in decimal form.

**Step 3: Compare Against the Publisher's CPM Offer**

$$
\text{Publisher's CPM Offer} = \text{₹}80 \text{ per 1,000 impressions}
$$

**Step 4: Comparative Analysis**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings via CPM deal} &= \text{eCPM}_{\text{CPC}} - \text{CPM}_{\text{offer}} \\
&= 90 - 80 \\
&= \text{₹}10 \text{ saved per 1,000 impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Decision Rule**

> **Conclusion:** The **publisher's flat CPM deal (₹80) is more cost-efficient** by ₹10 per 1,000 impressions than the CPC model (eCPM ₹90), *assuming the CTR remains 0.6%*. However, if creative optimisation lifts the CTR to **0.7%**, recompute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New eCPM} &= 15 \times 0.007 \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹}105 \text{ per 1,000 impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

> At a 0.7% CTR, the CPC deal becomes ₹25 *more expensive* per 1,000 impressions. **CTR is the strategic lever** — it determines which bid model wins.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Targeting Method Selection Decision Tree

The following matrix helps students select the optimal targeting method given a campaign objective (a frequent 7-mark question).

| Campaign Objective | Primary Targeting | Secondary Targeting | Bid Strategy | Expected KPI |
|--------------------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Brand Awareness** | Audience (Affinity / In-Market) | Demographic, Contextual | Target CPM / Maximize Reach | CPM, Reach, Frequency |
| **Website Traffic** | Audience + Placement (high-CTR sites) | Contextual | Manual CPC / Maximize Clicks | CPC, CTR |
| **Lead Generation** | In-Market Audiences + Lookalike | Demographic, Remarketing | Target CPA / Maximize Conversions | CPA, Conversion Rate |
| **E-commerce Sales** | Remarketing + Customer Match | In-Market, Demographic | Target ROAS / Maximize Conversion Value | ROAS, Revenue, AOV |
| **App Installs** | Audience (App-User Lookalikes) | Demographic, Placement (mobile apps) | Target Install Volume / tCPI | CPI, Install Rate |
| **Video Views** | In-Stream Placements + Affinity Audiences | Topic, Demographic | CPV / Target View Rate | VTR, CPV, Viewable Reach |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Heuristic:** The deeper the funnel stage, the narrower the targeting and the more conversion-optimised the bid strategy. Awareness = broad audiences + CPM; Conversion = remarketing + tCPA/tROAS.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Ad Rank Auction Mechanics

**Problem Statement:**
Two advertisers, **Alpha Ads** and **Beta Brands**, compete in the same GDN auction for a placement on ManoramaOnline.com. Their parameters are:

| Advertiser | Bid (₹ CPM) | Quality Score (1–10) | Uses Responsive Display Ads? |
|------------|-------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Alpha Ads | ₹120 | 7 | Yes |
| Beta Brands | ₹100 | 9 | No |

Determine the winner and explain the rationale.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1: Apply the Ad Rank Formula**

$$
\text{Ad Rank} = \text{Bid} \times \text{Quality Score} \times \text{Format Impact}
$$

**Step 2: Assume Format Impact Multipliers**

Google assigns the following approximate multipliers:

- Responsive Display Ad → **Format Impact = 1.10**
- Static Image Ad → **Format Impact = 1.00**

**Step 3: Compute Ad Rank for Alpha Ads**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ad Rank}_{\text{Alpha}} &= 120 \times 7 \times 1.10 \\
&= 840 \times 1.10 \\
&= 924
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Ad Rank for Beta Brands**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ad Rank}_{\text{Beta}} &= 100 \times 9 \times 1.00 \\
&= 900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compare and Conclude**

$$
\text{Ad Rank}_{\text{Alpha}} = 924 > \text{Ad Rank}_{\text{Beta}} = 900
$$

> **Conclusion:** **Alpha Ads wins** the auction despite bidding only marginally higher (₹120 vs ₹100), because its Quality Score gap (7 vs 9) was *offset* by the **Format Impact boost** of Responsive Display Ads. This is a textbook demonstration that **format choice and creative quality can compensate for higher bid prices** — a high-yield KTU conceptual takeaway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Pattern Note:** The Ad Rank formula appears in nearly every KTU Module 3 Display Campaign 14-mark question. Memorise the multiplication order: **Bid → Quality → Format Impact**. Reversing the order is a mark-losing error.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Display Campaign Workflow Architecture

This diagram maps the **end-to-end operational flow** of a Display Campaign from advertiser setup through auction to user impression delivery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Advertiser Initiates Campaign Setup] --> B[Select Campaign Objective]
    B --> C[Define Budget and Bid Strategy]
    C --> D[Configure Geo, Language, Device Targeting]
    D --> E[Build Ad Groups with Targeting Layers]
    E --> F[Upload Creatives - Images Headlines Logo]
    F --> G[Campaign Goes Live - Enters RTB Auction Pool]
    G --> H{User Visits GDN Publisher Page}
    H --> I[Publisher SSP Fires Ad Request]
    I --> J[Google Ad Exchange Conducts Real Time Auction]
    J --> K[Calculate Ad Rank per Advertiser]
    K --> L{Ad Rank Exceeds Threshold}
    L -->|Yes| M[Winning Creative Serves to User]
    L -->|No| N[Next Ranked Ad Considered]
    M --> O[User Views or Interacts with Ad]
    O --> P{User Action}
    P -->|Click| Q[Redirect to Landing Page]
    P -->|View Only| R[Record Impression and Viewability]
    Q --> S[Conversion Tracking via Pixel]
    R --> T[Data Fed Back to ML Optimisation Engine]
    S --> T
    T --> U[Performance Insights Dashboard Update]
    U --> V{Performance Meets KPI Targets}
    V -->|Yes| W[Continue and Scale Campaign]
    V -->|No| X[Optimise Creatives Bid or Targeting]
    X --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The loop from `X` (Optimise) back to `E` (Build Ad Groups) represents the **continuous optimisation cycle** — Display Campaigns are *never* "set-and-forget". KTU examiners credit students who mention iterative optimisation in their 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Campaign Hierarchy Block Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[ACCOUNT - Advertiser Master Container] --> B1[CAMPAIGN 1 - Brand Awareness]
    A1 --> B2[CAMPAIGN 2 - Remarketing]
    A1 --> B3[CAMPAIGN 3 - App Promotion]

    B1 --> C1[Ad Group - In-Market Audience]
    B1 --> C2[Ad Group - Affinity Audience]
    B1 --> C3[Ad Group - Demographic Filter]

    B2 --> C4[Ad Group - Cart Abandoners 7 Days]
    B2 --> C5[Ad Group - Site Visitors 30 Days]

    B3 --> C6[Ad Group - Lookalike App Users]
    B3 --> C7[Ad Group - Mobile App Placement]

    C1 --> D1[Responsive Display Ad Variant A]
    C1 --> D2[Responsive Display Ad Variant B]
    C1 --> D3[Static Image Ad - 300x250]

    C4 --> D4[Dynamic Remarketing Banner]
    C4 --> D5[15 Percent Discount Creative]

    C7 --> D6[Video Ad - 15 Sec Skippable]
    C7 --> D7[Playable App Demo Ad]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters:** It demonstrates the **3-tier (Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Ad)** hierarchy that KTU examiners test. Note how one **Account** contains multiple **Campaigns**; each Campaign contains multiple **Ad Groups**; each Ad Group bundles multiple **Ad variants** for ML rotation.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Display Targeting Method Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Display Campaign Targeting Methods] --> Q[Audience Based]
    P --> R[Content Based]
    P --> S[Placement Based]
    P --> T[Demographic Based]
    P --> U[Remarketing Based]

    Q --> Q1[Affinity - Interest Based]
    Q --> Q2[In-Market - Active Buyers]
    Q --> Q3[Life Events - Marriage Job Change]
    Q --> Q4[Lookalike - Similar to Existing Customers]

    R --> R1[Keyword Contextual]
    R --> R2[Topic Targeting]
    R --> R3[Category of Page]

    S --> S1[Managed Placements - Manual Selection]

    T --> T1[Age Range]
    T --> T2[Gender]

```

> [!NOTE]

Each targeting method is a *node* splitting the user-base into progressively more refined sub-populations, analogous to the hierarchical segmentation performed by supervised ML models like Random Forest or CART.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Display Ad Auction Sequence (RTB)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as User Browser
    participant PubSite as Publisher Site
    participant SSP as Publisher SSP
    participant AdEx as Ad Exchange
    participant DSP as Advertiser DSP
    participant AdServer as Ad Server Database

    User->>PubSite: Loads Page
    PubSite->>SSP: Ad Slot Available Signal
    SSP->>AdEx: Auction Request with User Context
    AdEx->>DSP: Bid Request to Multiple Advertisers
    DSP->>AdEx: Submit Bid and Creative in <120ms
    AdEx->>DSP: Conduct Second Price Auction
    DSP->>AdEx: Winner Notified
    AdEx->>PubSite: Winning Creative URL
    PubSite->>User: Render Ad on Page
    User->>PubSite: Click or Skip Ad
    PubSite->>AdServer: Log Impression and Click Event
    AdServer->>DSP: Update Performance Counters
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 120ms RTB Rule:** The entire auction cycle — from bid request to creative render — must complete in **under 120 milliseconds** to avoid degrading user experience. This is the engineering SLA that Display Campaign infrastructure is built around. KTU questions on "programmatic advertising" or "real-time bidding" require mentioning this latency constraint.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define Display Campaigns. List any four ad formats supported by the Google Display Network.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Expected, 80–100 words):**

A Display Campaign is a paid digital advertising model that delivers visually engaging creatives — such as static banners, animated images, rich media, and video units — across a vast network of publisher websites, mobile applications, and video partner properties (collectively the Google Display Network, or GDN), targeting audiences based on demographics, interests, behaviour, or context rather than active search queries.

**Four Ad Formats:**
1. **Static Image Ads** — JPG/PNG/GIF banners in IAB-standard sizes (300×250, 728×90, etc.).
2. **Animated GIF / HTML5 Ads** — Interactive banners with animation up to 30 seconds.
3. **Responsive Display Ads (RDA)** — Auto-generated combinations from uploaded assets optimised by Google ML.
4. **Video Ads** — Skippable in-stream, non-skippable in-stream (15 sec), bumper (6 sec).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is Remarketing in Display Advertising? Why is it considered the highest-ROAS targeting method?**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Expected, 80–100 words):**

Remarketing is a Display Campaign targeting method that re-engages users who have previously visited the advertiser's website or mobile app but did not convert, by serving them tailored creatives as they browse the Google Display Network. It uses cookie-based or list-based audience segmentation.

**Why it delivers the highest ROAS:**
Remarketing targets users with **prior demonstrated intent** (they have already shown interest). This *warmer audience* converts at significantly higher rates than cold audiences, lowering CPA and elevating ROAS. Industry benchmarks show remarketing lists convert **2–3× higher** than prospecting audiences, making it the most efficient use of Display spend for performance-focused advertisers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B, students typically lose marks by (1) writing generic definitions without numerical examples, (2) confusing *Reach* with *Impressions*, (3) forgetting to state the unit of CPM as *per 1000 impressions* not *per impression*, and (4) omitting the **Format Impact** term from the Ad Rank formula. Marks are awarded for **formula correctness, step-by-step derivation, and numerical finality**.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**Q: Display Campaigns and Their Performance Metrics.**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the complete anatomy of a Display Campaign — including its hierarchy, the various targeting methods available, and the bid strategies a marketer can choose from. Use a neat diagram to illustrate your answer.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A Display Campaign delivers **5,00,000 impressions** and generates **4,000 clicks** and **80 conversions**. The total cost is **₹60,000**. Calculate the **CPM, CPC, CTR, and CPA**. If the average order value per conversion is ₹1,200, determine the **ROAS** and comment on the campaign's profitability.

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (a) [7 Marks]

A Display Campaign follows a **3-tier hierarchy**: **Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Ad**. The Campaign level defines the **objective** (Brand Awareness, Consideration, Conversion), **budget** (daily or total), **bid strategy** (CPC, CPM, CPA, tROAS, Maximize Conversions), and **geo-language targeting**. The Ad Group level bundles **targeting criteria** with **creative assets**.

The five primary **targeting methods** are:

1. **Audience Targeting** — Interests, behaviours, in-market signals.
2. **Contextual Targeting** — Page-content keywords and topics.
3. **Placement Targeting** — Hand-picked publisher URLs and apps.
4. **Demographic Targeting** — Age, gender, income, parental status.
5. **Remarketing Targeting** — Re-engaging past visitors via cookie lists.

The **bid strategies** include Manual CPC, Enhanced CPC, Target CPA, Target ROAS, Maximize Conversions, Maximize Conversion Value, Viewable CPM (vCPM), and Target Impression Share. Modern campaigns increasingly rely on **automated, ML-driven smart bidding** (tCPA, tROAS) where Google's algorithm adjusts bids per auction based on conversion probability.

*(Diagram: Refer to Section 4.2 hierarchy diagram for full marks.)*

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impressions} &= 5{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Clicks} &= 4{,}000 \\
\text{Conversions} &= 80 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{₹}60{,}000 \\
\text{Average Order Value (AOV)} &= \text{₹}1{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — CTR Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CTR} &= \frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 0.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CPC Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPC} &= \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Clicks}} \\
&= \frac{60{,}000}{4{,}000} \\
&= \text{₹}15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CPA Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPA} &= \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Conversions}} \\
&= \frac{60{,}000}{80} \\
&= \text{₹}750
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — CPM Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPM} &= \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{60{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹}120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Revenue and ROAS Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue} &= \text{Conversions} \times \text{AOV} \\
&= 80 \times 1{,}200 \\
&= \text{₹}96{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROAS} &= \frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{96{,}000}{60{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 160\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Profitability Comment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= \text{Revenue} - \text{Cost} \\
&= 96{,}000 - 60{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}36{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The campaign achieves a **ROAS of 160%** (₹1.60 returned per ₹1 spent) with a **net profit of ₹36,000**. The CPA (₹750) is **37.5% lower** than the AOV (₹1,200), confirming positive unit economics. The CTR of **0.8%** falls within the GDN healthy benchmark (0.5%–1%), indicating acceptable creative relevance. **Verdict: Profitable campaign; recommend scaling budget by 30–50%.**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q: Advanced Display Advertising Strategies.**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare **CPC bidding** and **CPM bidding** for Display Campaigns. Under what conditions should a marketer prefer one over the other? Use the eCPM bridge formula to justify your reasoning with a numerical example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of **Programmatic Advertising** and **Real-Time Bidding (RTB)**. Describe the role of DSPs, SSPs, and Ad Exchanges in the programmatic ecosystem with a suitable block diagram.

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**CPC vs CPM — Conceptual Comparison:**

**Cost Per Click (CPC):** The advertiser pays only when a user clicks the ad. Risk of click-fraud exists, but the cost is tied directly to engagement. Best suited for **performance and traffic-generation** campaigns.

**Cost Per Mille (CPM):** The advertiser pays per 1,000 impressions regardless of clicks. Optimal for **brand awareness and reach campaigns** where exposure is the primary KPI, not clicks.

**eCPM Bridge — Numerical Justification:**

Consider a Display Campaign with the following:

- **CPC Bid:** ₹18
- **Achieved CTR:** 0.5% (= 0.005 in decimal)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{eCPM} &= \text{CPC} \times \text{CTR} \times 1000 \\
&= 18 \times 0.005 \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹}90
\end{aligned}
$$

If a publisher offers a flat CPM rate of ₹100, the **CPC model is cheaper** (effective ₹90 < ₹100 CPM). However, if creative optimisation raises CTR to **0.7%**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{New eCPM} &= 18 \times 0.007 \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹}126
\end{aligned}
$$

Now the CPC model is **more expensive** than the flat CPM offer. **Decision Rule:** Use CPC when CTR is below the break-even threshold; use CPM when CTR is sufficiently high or when campaign goal is awareness.

**Optimal Strategy Selection:**

- **CPC preferred** when: campaign objective is traffic, conversion tracking is mature, CTR is variable or low, budget is performance-tied.
- **CPM preferred** when: campaign objective is awareness, reach is the KPI, premium placements are targeted, creatives are guaranteed viewable.

#### MODEL ANSWER — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Programmatic Advertising:** The automated, real-time purchase and sale of digital ad inventory using machine learning algorithms and bidding technologies, eliminating manual insertion orders and human negotiation.

**Real-Time Bidding (RTB):** A programmatic auction mechanism in which ad impressions are bought and sold on a per-impression basis via instantaneous auctions that complete in **<120 milliseconds** before a webpage loads.

**Ecosystem Roles:**

- **DSP (Demand-Side Platform):** Advertiser-side software (e.g., DV360, The Trade Desk) that allows media buyers to manage multiple ad-exchange accounts and purchase inventory through a single interface, using audience data and ML-driven bid optimisation.
- **SSP (Supply-Side Platform):** Publisher-side software (e.g., Google Ad Manager) that enables publishers to sell ad inventory to multiple ad exchanges simultaneously, maximising yield.
- **Ad Exchange:** A digital marketplace (e.g., Google AdX, AppNexus) that conducts the real-time auction between DSPs and SSPs.
- **DMP (Data Management Platform):** Aggregates first-, second-, and third-party audience data to inform DSP bidding decisions.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Advertiser] → [DSP] → [Ad Exchange] ← [SSP] ← [Publisher Site]
                      ↓
              [Real-Time Auction]
                      ↓
              [Winning Ad Served to User]
```

> The ad request originates from the publisher, travels through the SSP to the ad exchange, which solicits bids from multiple DSPs, selects the winner in <120ms, and returns the creative for rendering — all before the user's browser fully loads the page.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. Writing "CPC is always cheaper than CPM" — wrong; it depends on CTR. Use the eCPM bridge.
> 2. Saying "RTB takes a few seconds" — wrong; it is **under 120 milliseconds**.
> 3. Confusing **DSP** (advertiser-side) with **SSP** (publisher-side). This is a 2-mark deduction error.
> 4. Forgetting to mention the **second-price auction** mechanism used by most ad exchanges (winner pays 2nd-highest bid + ₹0.01).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- ✅ **Display Campaign Definition:** Paid visual advertising across a network of third-party publisher sites, apps, and video properties targeting **passive audiences** based on demographics, interests, context, or past behaviour.
- ✅ **Hierarchy:** **Account → Campaign → Ad Group → Ad** (memorise this exact order).
- ✅ **Five Targeting Methods:** Audience, Contextual, Placement, Demographic, Remarketing.
- ✅ **GDN Reach:** 2M+ websites, apps, and video properties (Google's claim — examination-safe).
- ✅ **CPM Formula:** $\text{CPM} = (\text{Cost} / \text{Impressions}) \times 1000$ — denominator is *always* 1,000.
- ✅ **CPC Formula:** $\text{CPC} = \text{Cost} / \text{Clicks}$.
- ✅ **CTR Formula:** $\text{CTR} = (\text{Clicks} / \text{Impressions}) \times 100$.
- ✅ **CPA Formula:** $\text{CPA} = \text{Cost} / \text{Conversions}$.
- ✅ **eCPM Bridge:** $\text{eCPM} = \text{CPC} \times \text{CTR} \times 1000$ — enables CPC ↔ CPM comparison.
- ✅ **ROAS Formula:** $\text{ROAS} = (\text{Revenue} / \text{Cost}) \times 100$ — benchmark profitability threshold is typically **> 100%** (4:1 is best-in-class).
- ✅ **Ad Rank:** $\text{Bid} \times \text{Quality Score} \times \text{Format Impact}$.
- ✅ **RDA = Responsive Display Ads** (NOT Responsive Search Ads — different product).
- ✅ **Programmatic RTB Latency SLA:** < **120 milliseconds** end-to-end auction.
- ✅ **DSP = Demand-Side (Advertiser); SSP = Supply-Side (Publisher)**.
- ✅ **Auction Type:** Second-price auction (winner pays 2nd-highest bid + ₹0.01).
- ✅ **Remarketing = Highest ROAS method** (warm audience, prior intent).
- ✅ **Industry CTR Benchmark (GDN):** 0.5% – 1% (healthy range).
- ✅ **Continuous Optimisation:** Display Campaigns require iterative refinement of **creative + bid + targeting** — never set-and-forget.
- ✅ **Funnel Mapping:** Awareness → Display broad; Conversion → Display remarketing + tROAS.

> **End of Module 3 — Display Campaigns Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Video Campaigns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET833 - DIGITAL MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Video Campaigns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Video Campaign** in digital marketing is a structured, goal-oriented advertising initiative that uses **video creative assets** (moving images, audio, text overlays, and storytelling) to deliver promotional, educational, or brand-building messages to a defined audience across digital platforms such as **YouTube**, **Facebook**, **Instagram**, **LinkedIn**, **Twitter (X)**, and programmatic video networks.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framing of Digital Marketing, a video campaign is classified as a *paid media execution layer* that operates within the broader **RACE Framework** (Reach, Act, Convert, Engage) and is tightly integrated with **search, display, and social media campaigns** under a unified marketing funnel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** A Video Campaign is *any paid initiative that uses video content as the primary creative unit to achieve a measurable marketing objective (awareness, consideration, or conversion) within a defined budget and timeline.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Video Campaign** like a **movie trailer for your business** — but instead of being shown only in cinemas, it gets aired on every "TV screen" your audience looks at (their phones while commuting, laptops at work, smart TVs at home).

**Real-World Analogy — The Street Food Vendor:**

Imagine a street food vendor in Kerala who wants to attract more customers.

1. **Without a video campaign:** He just stands at his cart and waits for people to notice the aroma.
2. **With a video campaign:** He makes a 30-second reel of his samosa being fried crisp, posts it on Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts, runs it as a paid ad targeting people within a 5 km radius who love snacks — and his counter gets crowded within hours.

The **video** is the *creative hook*, the **platform** is the *stage*, the **targeting settings** are the *megaphone direction*, and the **bidding strategy** is the *budget he is willing to spend per interested viewer*.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Used in KTU Boards

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms are **frequently asked verbatim** in KTU University Examinations under Module 3.

- **TrueView Ads:** Skippable in-stream video ads where the advertiser pays only when the viewer watches at least **30 seconds** (or the entire ad if it is shorter than 30 seconds).
- **Bumper Ads:** Non-skippable **6-second** video ads ideal for mobile-first reach and quick message recall.
- **In-Stream Ads:** Ads that play before, during, or after video content on platforms like YouTube.
- **Discovery Ads:** Video ads that appear in YouTube search results, watch next, and home feed (formerly known as *In-Display* or *In-Feed* video ads).
- **Outstream Ads:** Video ads that appear *outside* video content (e.g., within articles or feed placements).
- **Masthead Ads:** Premium, full-screen, above-the-fold YouTube homepage ads available on a reserved basis.
