# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Integrated Digital Marketing. List any four key digital marketing channels.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Integrated Digital Marketing is the strategic alignment and coordination of all online marketing channels (SEO, SEM, Social Media, Email, Content, Mobile) to deliver a unified brand message and seamless customer experience.
- **Four Channels (0.375 each = 1.5 marks):**
  1. Search Engine Optimization (SEO)
  2. Search Engine Marketing / Paid Ads (SEM)
  3. Social Media Marketing (SMM)
  4. Email Marketing
- *Alternative accepted:* Content Marketing, Mobile Marketing, Affiliate Marketing, Influencer Marketing.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Multichannel, Cross-Channel, and Omnichannel marketing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per distinction):**

| Concept | Distinguishing Feature |
|---------|------------------------|
| **Multichannel** | Brand uses multiple channels, but each operates **independently** with no data sharing. |
| **Cross-Channel** | Customer movement **between channels is tracked** and analyzed, though experience is not fully unified. |
| **Omnichannel** | Channels are **fully integrated** through a central data platform, providing a single, seamless, continuous customer experience across all touchpoints. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write Multichannel and Cross-Channel as if they are the same. The board specifically tests the **"data-sharing and unified experience"** angle — failing to mention this distinction costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024]`

**"Integrated Digital Marketing is essential for delivering a unified customer experience in the modern business environment."**
**(a)** Explain the eight-step framework for integrating digital marketing channels. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the role of Customer Data Platform (CDP) and unified analytics in enabling integration, with a real-time example. **(7 marks)**

### Question A — Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Eight-Step Integration Framework (7 Marks)

1. **Channel Audit (1 mark):** Inventory all existing digital channels (SEO, SEM, social, email, mobile, content). Assess performance, audience overlap, gaps, and redundancies. Output: a Channel Audit Report identifying underperforming and high-potential channels.

2. **Define SMART Goals (0.5 mark):** Establish Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound objectives aligned with business KPIs (e.g., increase qualified leads by 25% in Q2).

3. **Customer Journey Mapping (1 mark):** Document all touchpoints from awareness to advocacy. Identify decision moments, pain points, and channel preferences. Use tools like Google Analytics behavior flow, Hotjar heatmaps, and buyer persona research.

4. **Channel Selection & Mix (1 mark):** Match channels to journey stages and audience behavior. Avoid spreading budget thin; use the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** — focus on channels delivering 80% of conversions.

5. **Content Alignment & Atomization (1 mark):** Create **pillar content** (e.g., a long-form blog or video) and atomize it into channel-specific assets (Instagram reels, LinkedIn posts, email snippets, infographics).

6. **Data Unification via CDP (1 mark):** Implement a Customer Data Platform to merge CRM, web, social, and email data into a **Single Customer View (SCV)**. Tools: Segment, Salesforce CDP, HubSpot.

7. **Integrated Campaign Execution (0.5 mark):** Launch synchronized campaigns with shared creative, consistent messaging, and coordinated timing across all chosen channels.

8. **Measurement, Attribution & Optimization (1 mark):** Use multi-touch attribution (MTA) models — linear, time-decay, position-based, or data-driven — to assign credit accurately. Iterate monthly using dashboards (Google Data Studio, Looker).

#### Part (b) — Role of CDP and Unified Analytics (7 Marks)

**Definition & Function (2 marks):**
A **Customer Data Platform (CDP)** is a packaged software that creates a persistent, unified customer database accessible to other systems. It collects data from **first-, second-, and third-party sources** — website behavior, CRM, mobile app events, social interactions, purchase history, and offline transactions — and stitches them into a **single customer profile** identified by a unique ID (email, phone, or device ID).

**Role in Integration (3 marks):**
- **360° Customer View:** Eliminates data silos by unifying fragmented customer data.
- **Real-Time Personalization:** Enables personalized content delivery across web, email, and ads in real time.
- **Cross-Channel Journey Tracking:** Maps the customer's path across devices and platforms.
- **Improved Attribution:** Provides accurate multi-touch attribution by linking all interactions to one customer ID.
- **Segmentation & Targeting:** Allows precise audience segmentation for paid media, email, and on-site personalization.

**Real-Time Example (2 marks):**
**Amazon** uses a sophisticated CDP architecture to integrate browsing history, wishlist items, purchase data, and Alexa voice queries. When a user adds a product to the cart on mobile but does not purchase, the CDP triggers a **personalized email within 2 hours**, a **retargeting ad on Instagram**, and a **push notification on the app** — all with the exact product image and a time-bound discount. This omnichannel orchestration, powered by unified data, contributes to Amazon's industry-leading **15–20% repeat purchase rate**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For 7-mark questions, students often write generic definitions. The board expects **specific tool names, frameworks, and numerical examples**. Citing Amazon, HubSpot, Segment, or Google Analytics 4 (GA4) adds 1–2 marks. Avoid writing more than 2 pages without structure — use **headings and bullet points** for clarity.

---

### Question B (Option 2) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A startup spends ₹3,00,000 on an integrated digital marketing campaign combining SEO, Google Ads, and Social Media. The campaign generated ₹12,00,000 in revenue and acquired 1,500 new customers. The average order value is ₹800, purchase frequency is 5 per year, and average customer lifespan is 2 years. **Calculate the ROMI, CAC, CLV, and CLV: CAC ratio. Comment on whether the integrated strategy is justified.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a 6-month integrated digital marketing plan for a new sustainable fashion brand targeting Gen Z in Kerala, including channel selection, content strategy, and KPIs. **(7 marks)**

### Question B — Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Numerical Problem Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate ROMI (1.5 marks)**

$$\text{ROMI} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\text{ROMI} = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 300\%$$

The campaign returned **₹3 for every ₹1 spent**.

**Step 2: Calculate CAC (1.5 marks)**

$$\text{CAC} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1{,}500} = \text{₹}\,200$$

Each new customer cost **₹200** to acquire.

**Step 3: Calculate CLV (2 marks)**

$$\text{CLV} = 800 \times 5 \times 2 = \text{₹}\,8{,}000$$

Each customer is worth **₹8,000** over their lifetime.

**Step 4: Calculate CLV: CAC Ratio (1 mark)**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{8{,}000}{200} = 40:1$$

**Step 5: Justification Comment (1 mark)**

The CLV:CAC ratio of **40:1** is far above the industry benchmark of **3:1**, indicating exceptional efficiency. However, an excessively high ratio (above 5:1) may suggest **under-investment in growth** — the startup could scale its marketing budget to acquire more customers. The integrated strategy is **justified and should be scaled**, with a recommendation to increase spend by 30–40% in the next quarter.

---

#### Part (b) — 6-Month Integrated Marketing Plan (7 Marks)

**Brand Context (0.5 mark):** Sustainable fashion brand, Kerala market, Gen Z audience (18–28 years).

**Month 1: Foundation & Audit (0.5 mark)**
- Set up GA4, Meta Pixel, Google Ads conversion tracking.
- Conduct competitor analysis and audience research (Instagram, YouTube Shorts trends).
- Define brand voice, visual identity, and sustainability narrative.

**Month 2: Content & SEO Setup (1 mark)**
- Launch website with SEO-optimized content: "Sustainable fashion Kerala," "Eco-friendly fabrics India."
- Begin keyword-targeted blogs (2 per week).
- Build backlink strategy through sustainability forums and Kerala influencer collaborations.

**Month 3: Social Media Launch (1 mark)**
- Activate Instagram, YouTube Shorts, and LinkedIn.
- Content pillars: behind-the-scenes, sustainability education, customer styling reels, founder story.
- Influencer partnerships with 5–10 micro-influencers (10K–50K followers) in Kerala.

**Month 4: Paid Campaigns & Email (1 mark)**
- Google Search Ads on high-intent keywords.
- Meta Ads retargeting visitors who browsed but did not purchase.
- Launch email newsletter with a 10% first-purchase incentive.

**Month 5: Community & UGC (1 mark)**
- Launch a "Style It Sustainably" UGC contest on Instagram.
- Customer testimonials, sustainability certifications display.
- Pop-up store or offline experience in Kochi/Trivandrum.

**Month 6: Analytics, Optimization & Scale (1 mark)**
- Review full-funnel metrics: traffic, engagement, conversion, CLV.
- Apply multi-touch attribution to refine channel mix.
- Plan festive-season push (Onam, Christmas collections).

**KPI Dashboard (1 mark):**

| KPI | Target (6 months) |
|-----|-------------------|
| Website Traffic | 50,000 monthly visitors |
| Social Followers | 25,000 (Instagram) |
| Email List | 10,000 subscribers |
| Conversion Rate | 2.5% |
| CLV:CAC | 4:1 |
| ROMI | 250% |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For 7-mark design questions, students often describe channels generically. The board awards marks for **specificity** — name platforms, give content types, define quantifiable KPIs, and tie everything to the target audience (Gen Z, Kerala). Generic answers score a maximum of 4–5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY KTU EXAM POINTS — MODULE 4**

- **Definition:** Integrated Digital Marketing = unified, customer-centric coordination of all digital channels.
- **Three Models:** Multichannel (independent) → Cross-Channel (tracked movement) → Omnichannel (fully unified).
- **Framework:** 8 Steps — Audit → Goals → Journey → Channel Mix → Content → Data Unify → Execute → Measure.
- **Core Tools:** CDP (Customer Data Platform), CRM, GA4, HubSpot, Segment, Salesforce.
- **Customer Journey Stages:** Awareness → Consideration → Decision → Purchase → Retention → Advocacy.
- **Key Metrics (must memorize formulas):**
  - ROMI, CAC, CLV, CLV:CAC, CTR, CVR, CPC, Bounce Rate, MER.
- **Ideal CLV:CAC Ratio = 3:1**; below 1:1 = loss; above 5:1 = under-investment.
- **Pareto Principle:** 80% of conversions come from 20% of channels — focus and integrate them.
- **Content Atomization:** One pillar content → multiple channel-specific derivatives.
- **Attribution Models:** Last-click, First-click, Linear, Time-Decay, Position-Based, Data-Driven.
- **Brand Consistency:** Same voice, tone, colors, and messaging across all touchpoints.
- **Data Privacy:** GDPR, DPDP Act (India 2023), CCPA — must be cited in 14-mark answers.
- **Integration ≠ More Channels:** It is about **strategic alignment**, not just being present everywhere.
- **Common Mistake:** Treating SEO, SEM, and SMM as separate — they are **interdependent** in modern funnels.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring Digital Marketing Success
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET833 - DIGITAL MARKETING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measuring Digital Marketing Success

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Measuring Digital Marketing Success** is the systematic process of tracking, analysing, and evaluating Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) across digital channels to quantify the effectiveness, efficiency, and profitability of online marketing campaigns. It involves the use of web analytics platforms, attribution models, and performance dashboards to convert raw user data into actionable business insights, ensuring alignment with organisational Return on Investment (ROI) objectives.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic bridges the operational planning modules with strategic business outcomes, mandating that students understand not only *what* to measure, but *why* specific metrics are chosen for specific business goals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Standard):**
> Digital Marketing Measurement = The continuous monitoring of digital campaign metrics (KPIs) using analytics tools to evaluate performance against predefined business goals, expressed quantitatively through ROI, ROAS, CPA, and conversion ratios.

### Intuitive Conceptual Analogy
Think of **Measuring Digital Marketing Success** like a **GPS Navigation System for a road trip**:

- Your **business goal** is the destination address.
- Your **digital campaigns** are the routes you take (Facebook, Google, Email, SEO).
- **KPIs and metrics** are the speedometer, fuel gauge, and traffic signals — they tell you whether you are moving in the right direction, burning too much fuel, or stuck in traffic.
- The **analytics dashboard** is your GPS screen, showing live data so you can reroute when something is not working.
- Without measurement, you are driving blind — you might be moving, but you have no idea if you are heading toward profit or loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):**
> Measurement is the *closing loop* of the digital marketing cycle. Without it, the **Plan → Execute → Measure → Optimise** framework collapses into guesswork.

### Standard Performance Metrics Vocabulary

The following core metrics must be memorised for KTU examinations:

- **KPI (Key Performance Indicator):** A quantifiable measure used to evaluate success.
- **CTR (Click-Through Rate):** Ratio of users who click on a link to those who view it.
- **CR (Conversion Rate):** Percentage of visitors who complete a desired action.
- **CPA (Cost Per Acquisition):** Total cost divided by the number of conversions.
- **CPC (Cost Per Click):** Cost incurred for each click on a paid advertisement.
- **CPM (Cost Per Mille):** Cost per one thousand impressions.
- **ROAS (Return on Advertising Spend):** Revenue generated per unit of ad spend.
- **ROI (Return on Investment):** Net profit relative to the total investment cost.
- **Bounce Rate:** Percentage of visitors who leave without any interaction.
- **LTV (Lifetime Value):** Total revenue a customer is expected to generate over their lifetime.
- **Engagement Rate:** Level of interaction (likes, shares, comments) relative to reach.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Operational Logic of Digital Marketing Measurement

Digital marketing measurement operates on a five-stage logical pipeline:

1. **Define Business Objectives:** Clarify whether the goal is awareness (top-of-funnel), consideration (mid-funnel), or conversion (bottom-of-funnel). The KTU model uses the **AIDA** framework — Awareness, Interest, Desire, Action.
2. **Select Relevant KPIs:** Each objective maps to a specific KPI family. *Example:* Brand Awareness → Impressions, Reach, CPM; Conversion → ROAS, CPA, Conversion Rate.
3. **Implement Tracking Infrastructure:** Deploy pixels (Meta Pixel), tags (Google Ads Tag), UTM parameters, and analytics platforms (Google Analytics 4).
4. **Collect & Aggregate Data:** Channel-level data is centralised in dashboards (Looker Studio, Tableau) for holistic analysis.
5. **Analyse, Attribute & Optimise:** Use attribution models (Last-Click, Linear, Time-Decay, Position-Based, Data-Driven) to assign credit, then reallocate budget to the highest-performing channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Measurement:**
> Marketing without measurement is a financial black hole. Measurement transforms marketing from a **cost centre** into a **revenue-generating investment**, justifying budgets to stakeholders and enabling data-driven scaling.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises every formula a KTU student must master for ESE questions on this topic.

| Metric | Formula | Inputs Required | Output Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CTR** | $\text{Clicks} \div \text{Impressions} \times 100$ | Total Clicks, Impressions | Percentage (%) |
| **Conversion Rate (CR)** | $\text{Conversions} \div \text{Total Visitors} \times 100$ | Conversions, Visitor Count | Percentage (%) |
| **CPC** | $\text{Total Ad Spend} \div \text{Total Clicks}$ | Ad Spend, Clicks | Currency per Click |
| **CPA** | $\text{Total Ad Spend} \div \text{Total Acquisitions}$ | Ad Spend, Conversions | Currency per Acquisition |
| **CPM** | $(\text{Total Ad Spend} \div \text{Total Impressions}) \times 1000$ | Ad Spend, Impressions | Currency per 1000 Impressions |
| **ROAS** | $\text{Revenue from Ads} \div \text{Ad Spend}$ | Revenue, Ad Spend | Ratio (e.g., 4:1) |
| **ROI** | $((\text{Revenue} - \text{Cost}) \div \text{Cost}) \times 100$ | Revenue, Total Cost | Percentage (%) |
| **Engagement Rate** | $(\text{Total Engagements} \div \text{Total Reach}) \times 100$ | Engagements, Reach | Percentage (%) |
| **Bounce Rate** | $(\text{Single Page Visits} \div \text{Total Entries}) \times 100$ | Bounces, Entries | Percentage (%) |
| **LTV : CAC Ratio** | $\text{LTV} \div \text{CAC}$ | Lifetime Value, Acquisition Cost | Ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> **ROAS** measures efficiency of *advertising* spend only, expressed as a ratio. **ROI** measures profitability of *total investment* (including production, salaries, tools), expressed as a percentage. ROAS is a subset of ROI in campaign-level analysis.

### Real-World Utility in Marketing Engineering

In practice, these formulas drive automated bidding algorithms in Google Ads and Meta Ads Manager. Smart Bidding strategies like **Target CPA** and **Maximize Conversions** use these exact formulas in real-time to optimise campaigns. Digital Marketing analysts use them to construct **Marketing Mix Models (MMM)** and incrementality tests that determine whether a 20% sales lift was caused by an ad campaign or by seasonal demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Derivations for KTU Numerical Problems

The following exhaustive numerical walkthroughs reflect the *exact* evaluation style used by KTU board examiners.

---

### Derivation 1: ROI Calculation for a Multi-Channel Campaign

**Problem Statement:**
A company invests in a digital campaign with the following breakdown: Google Ads spend = ₹50,000; Facebook Ads spend = ₹30,000; Content Marketing cost = ₹20,000. The campaign generates revenue of ₹2,50,000. Calculate the **ROI**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Total Cost (Investment).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{Google Ads Spend} + \text{Facebook Ads Spend} + \text{Content Marketing Cost} \\
&= 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 20{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the Total Revenue Generated.**

$$
\text{Total Revenue} = 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Net Profit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit} &= \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Cost} \\
&= 2{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the ROI Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \left(\frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Cost}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 1.5 \times 100 \\
&= 150\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The campaign delivered a **150% ROI**, meaning for every ₹1 invested, the company earned ₹1.50 in profit. This is well above the KTU benchmark of 100% for healthy campaigns.

---

### Derivation 2: ROAS vs CPA for a Google Ads Campaign

**Problem Statement:**
A Google Ads campaign records the following data over 30 days: Ad Spend = ₹40,000; Clicks = 2,000; Conversions = 100; Revenue from Conversions = ₹1,60,000. Calculate **CTR, CPC, CPA, Conversion Rate, and ROAS**.

**Step 1: Compute CTR (Assume Impressions = 50,000).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CTR} &= \left(\frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{2{,}000}{50{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute CPC.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPC} &= \frac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Clicks}} \\
&= \frac{40{,}000}{2{,}000} \\
&= 20 \text{ INR per click}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Conversion Rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CR} &= \left(\frac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Total Clicks}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{100}{2{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute CPA.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPA} &= \frac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Conversions}} \\
&= \frac{40{,}000}{100} \\
&= 400 \text{ INR per acquisition}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute ROAS.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROAS} &= \frac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{40{,}000} \\
&= 4 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 4:1 ROAS is considered excellent by industry standards (benchmark is typically 2:1 to 4:1). Combined with a 5% Conversion Rate and ₹400 CPA, this campaign is highly profitable.

---

### Derivation 3: LTV : CAC Ratio Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
An e-commerce company spends ₹60,000 on ads, acquires 200 customers. The average order value is ₹1,200, and each customer makes 3 purchases per year for 3 years. Calculate **CAC, LTV, and the LTV : CAC ratio**.

**Step 1: Compute CAC.**

$$
\text{CAC} = \frac{60{,}000}{200} = 300 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2: Compute LTV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LTV} &= \text{Average Order Value} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan} \\
&= 1{,}200 \times 3 \times 3 \\
&= 10{,}800 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute LTV : CAC Ratio.**

$$
\text{LTV : CAC} = \frac{10{,}800}{300} = 36 : 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Benchmark:** A healthy LTV : CAC ratio lies between **3 : 1 and 5 : 1**. A ratio of 36:1 indicates the company may be **under-investing** in acquisition, leaving profitable growth on the table.

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | Measures | Best Used For | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CTR** | Ad creative effectiveness | Ad Copy, Creative Testing | Does not measure post-click quality |
| **Conversion Rate** | Landing page + offer effectiveness | Funnel optimisation | Ignores traffic quality |
| **CPA** | Acquisition cost efficiency | Budget allocation | Does not reflect customer quality |
| **ROAS** | Ad revenue efficiency | Campaign-level scaling | Ignores profit margins |
| **ROI** | Total profitability | Strategic investment decisions | Requires full cost accounting |
| **LTV : CAC** | Long-term unit economics | SaaS, e-commerce viability | Requires accurate churn data |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The Digital Marketing Measurement Cycle

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the closed-loop measurement framework that connects campaign execution to strategic optimisation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Define Business Goal] --> nodeB[Step 2: Select KPIs]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Deploy Tracking Tools]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Launch Campaign]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 5: Collect Channel Data]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 6: Analyse via Dashboard]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Goal Achieved?}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Step 7: Scale Winning Channels]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeI[Step 8: A/B Test and Optimise]
    nodeI --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Final: Report ROI to Stakeholders]
```

### Diagram 2: Attribution Model Comparison

This block diagram maps the five standard attribution models used to assign conversion credit across customer touchpoints.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[First Interaction] --> nodeB[Linear]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Time Decay]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Position Based U Shaped]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Data Driven Algorithmic]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Last Click Default]

    subgraph models[Attribution Spectrum]
        nodeA
        nodeB
        nodeC
        nodeD
        nodeE
        nodeF
    end
```

### Diagram 3: Marketing Funnel with KPIs per Stage

This diagram demonstrates how different KPIs map to each stage of the marketing funnel, a concept that frequently appears in KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    top1[Awareness Stage] --> mid1[Consideration Stage]
    mid1 --> mid2[Conversion Stage]
    mid2 --> bot1[Retention Stage]

    top1 --> kpi1[KPI: Impressions, Reach, CPM]
    mid1 --> kpi2[KPI: CTR, Engagement Rate, Bounce Rate]
    mid2 --> kpi3[KPI: Conversion Rate, CPA, ROAS]
    bot1 --> kpi4[KPI: LTV, Repeat Purchase Rate, Churn]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: Define ROAS and explain how it differs from ROI. Why is the distinction important in digital marketing measurement?**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Answer]**

**ROAS (Return on Advertising Spend)** is a marketing efficiency metric that measures the gross revenue generated for every unit of currency spent on advertising. The formula is:

$$
\text{ROAS} = \frac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}}
$$

It is expressed as a ratio, such as **4 : 1**, meaning ₹4 revenue per ₹1 spent.

**ROI (Return on Investment)** is a profitability metric that evaluates net profit relative to the total investment, including ad spend, production costs, salaries, software tools, and overhead. The formula is:

$$
\text{ROI} = \left(\frac{\text{Revenue} - \text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Cost}}\right) \times 100
$$

It is expressed as a percentage.

The distinction is important because **ROAS is a campaign-level tactical metric** used by marketers to optimise ad budgets, while **ROI is a business-level strategic metric** used by executives to evaluate overall profitability. A campaign can have a high ROAS but low ROI if production and operational costs are high. Hence, ROAS must always be interpreted in the broader context of ROI for accurate decision-making.

---

**Question 2: What is a Conversion Funnel? List any three KPIs used to measure the conversion stage.**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Answer]**

A **Conversion Funnel** is a visual model representing the sequential stages a user passes through, from initial awareness of a brand to the final conversion action (purchase, sign-up, download). It typically includes four stages: **Awareness → Interest → Consideration → Conversion**.

Three KPIs used to measure the conversion stage are:

1. **Conversion Rate (CR):** Percentage of visitors who complete the desired action.
2. **Cost Per Acquisition (CPA):** Cost incurred to acquire one converted customer.
3. **Return on Ad Spend (ROAS):** Revenue earned per rupee of advertising investment.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, 14-Mark Module Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the key components of a digital marketing analytics framework. Discuss the role of Google Analytics 4 (GA4) in modern measurement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A startup launched a 45-day digital campaign. The data collected was as follows: Google Ads spend = ₹80,000 (Impressions = 2,00,000, Clicks = 8,000); Facebook Ads spend = ₹60,000 (Impressions = 1,50,000, Clicks = 6,000); Instagram Influencer cost = ₹40,000 (Conversions = 250). Total revenue from the campaign was ₹6,00,000. Calculate the **overall ROI, ROAS for Google Ads, and the blended CPA for the campaign**. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

A digital marketing analytics framework consists of **five key components**:

1. **Data Collection Layer:** Implementation of tracking tools such as GA4, Meta Pixel, UTM parameters, and server-side tags.
2. **Data Management Layer:** Centralised storage in data warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake) and ETL pipelines.
3. **Analysis Layer:** Statistical and behavioural analysis using tools like GA4, Adobe Analytics, and Looker Studio.
4. **Reporting Layer:** Custom dashboards that visualise KPIs for different stakeholders.
5. **Activation Layer:** Insights fed back into campaign tools for automated optimisation.

**GA4's role:** GA4 is Google's event-based analytics platform that uses an event-driven data model, enabling cross-platform tracking (web + app), privacy-first measurement using machine learning, and integration with BigQuery for advanced SQL analysis. It replaced Universal Analytics in 2023 and is now the industry standard.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Calculate Total Cost.**

$$
\text{Total Cost} = 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 1{,}80{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Overall ROI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \left(\frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(\frac{4{,}20{,}000}{1{,}80{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 233.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Google Ads ROAS.**
(Assuming all revenue is attributable to ads for this calculation — students must state this assumption explicitly in the exam.)

$$
\text{Google ROAS} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = 7.5 : 1
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Blended CPA.**

$$
\text{Blended CPA} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{250} = 720 \text{ INR}
$$

**Conclusion:** The campaign achieved an excellent ROI of 233%, a Google Ads ROAS of 7.5:1, and a blended CPA of ₹720, indicating highly profitable performance.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, 14-Mark Module Choice - Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **Attribution Modelling** in digital marketing. Compare the **Last-Click** and **Data-Driven** attribution models with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce company generated the following metrics for Q1: Website Visitors = 1,00,000; Add-to-Carts = 8,000; Checkouts Initiated = 3,000; Completed Purchases = 1,500; Total Ad Spend = ₹5,00,000; Total Revenue = ₹15,00,000. Calculate the **Funnel Drop-off Rate at each stage, the Overall Conversion Rate, and the ROI**. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Attribution Modelling** is the framework used to assign conversion credit to multiple marketing touchpoints a customer interacts with before converting. It answers the question: *Which channel deserves the credit for a sale?*

**Last-Click Attribution** assigns 100% credit to the final touchpoint before conversion.
- *Example:* A user clicks an Instagram ad → reads a blog → clicks a Google Ad → buys. Last-Click gives 100% credit to Google Ads, ignoring the Instagram and blog influence.
- *Limitation:* Ignores upper-funnel channels, leading to budget misallocation toward search ads.

**Data-Driven Attribution (DDA)** uses machine learning algorithms to dynamically distribute credit across touchpoints based on each interaction's actual contribution to conversions.
- *Example:* In the same customer journey, DDA might assign 30% to Instagram, 25% to the blog, and 45% to Google Ads, based on conversion probability uplift.
- *Advantage:* Provides a more accurate, evidence-based view of channel effectiveness, available natively in GA4.

The shift from Last-Click to DDA represents the industry's evolution toward privacy-first, AI-powered measurement in a cookieless future.

##### Model Solution for (b):

**Funnel Stages:** Visitors → Add-to-Cart → Checkout → Purchase.

**Step 1: Visitor to Add-to-Cart Drop-off.**

$$
\text{Drop-off}_1 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 92\%
$$

**Step 2: Add-to-Cart to Checkout Drop-off.**

$$
\text{Drop-off}_2 = \frac{8{,}000 - 3{,}000}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 62.5\%
$$

**Step 3: Checkout to Purchase Drop-off.**

$$
\text{Drop-off}_3 = \frac{3{,}000 - 1{,}500}{3{,}000} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 4: Overall Conversion Rate.**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{1{,}500}{1{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 1.5\%
$$

**Step 5: ROI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \left(\frac{15{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= 200\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The largest drop-off (92%) occurs at the top of the funnel, indicating poor landing page engagement. Despite this, the campaign delivered a healthy 200% ROI.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ROAS with ROI.** ROAS uses *Revenue*, ROI uses *Net Profit*. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this swap.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** in percentage-based formulas (CTR, CR, ROI). Always state the final unit as %.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** in attribution problems. If revenue allocation is arbitrary, explicitly mention "assuming proportional revenue distribution."
> 4. **Skipping the conclusion line.** Every numerical answer must end with an interpretive sentence ("The campaign is profitable / underperforming").
> 5. **Wrong formula for Bounce Rate.** It is single-page sessions divided by total sessions, NOT exits divided by sessions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CTR, CR, CPA, CPC, CPM** are *channel-level* performance metrics.
- **ROAS** is *campaign-level* revenue efficiency; **ROI** is *business-level* profitability.
- **LTV : CAC ratio** between **3 : 1 and 5 : 1** indicates a sustainable business model.
- **Attribution Models** include Last-Click, Linear, Time-Decay, Position-Based (U-Shaped), and Data-Driven.
- **GA4** uses an event-based, privacy-first, cross-platform data model — the current industry standard.
- **UTM parameters** (Source, Medium, Campaign, Term, Content) are essential for granular traffic analysis.
- Always **end numerical answers** with an interpretive conclusion.
- The measurement cycle is **closed-loop**: Define → Track → Analyse → Optimise → Repeat.
- Distinguish **micro-conversions** (add-to-cart, newsletter sign-up) from **macro-conversions** (purchase, subscription).
- A high **bounce rate** signals landing page relevance issues, not always poor ad targeting.
- **Marketing Mix Models (MMM)** complement attribution by measuring offline + online incrementality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Marketing Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEPET833 - DIGITAL MARKETING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Digital Marketing Case Studies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Digital Marketing Case Studies** are rigorously documented, evidence-based analyses of real-world business scenarios where organizations have strategically deployed digital channels — such as **Search Engine Optimization (SEO)**, **Search Engine Marketing (SEM)**, **Social Media Marketing (SMM)**, **Content Marketing**, **Email Marketing**, **Affiliate Marketing**, and **Mobile Marketing** — to achieve measurable business objectives. These case studies serve as pedagogical instruments within the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum to dissect strategic decision-making, campaign execution, performance metrics (**KPIs**), and return on investment (**ROI**) within digitally transformed business ecosystems. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, a case study is not merely a "story of a brand" — it is a structured analytical artifact that maps the **4Ps of Digital Marketing** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) and the **4Cs** (Consumer, Cost, Convenience, Communication) onto a measurable digital funnel (**TOFU → MOFU → BOFU**). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Surgeon's Clinical Case File" Analogy** > > Imagine a digital marketing campaign as a complex surgical operation. The case study is the *patient's clinical file* — a chronological record containing pre-operative symptoms (market problem), the surgeon's notes (strategy), instruments used (channels), vital signs during surgery (KPIs), and post-operative recovery (results). Just as a medical student learns surgery by studying actual operations, a digital marketing student masters strategy by dissecting **real campaigns with real numbers**. You are the consulting physician auditing the campaign's *vital signs*: **CTR (Click-Through Rate)**, **CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost)**, **LTV (Lifetime Value)**, and **Conversion Rate**. ### 1.3 The KTU 2024 Mandated Standard Metrics (Must Memorize) | Metric | Full Form | Standard Benchmark | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **CTR** | Click-Through Rate | **2–5%** (Industry Average) | | **CAC** | Customer Acquisition Cost | Varies by industry | | **ROAS** | Return on Ad Spend | **4:1** (Healthy) | | **LTV** | Customer Lifetime Value | LTV:CAC = **3:1** | | **Bounce Rate** | Single-page exits | **40–60%** (Average) | | **Engagement Rate** | Interactions/Reach | **1–3%** (Organic) | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently award partial marks simply for quoting *exact benchmark values* in case study answers. Memorize the **ROAS = 4:1** and **LTV:CAC = 3:1** golden ratios. ## 1.4 Why KTU Tests Case Studies The KTU 2024 Scheme (per **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Analyze the effectiveness of digital marketing strategies through real-world case studies"*) explicitly requires students to: 1. **Identify** the digital marketing problem/challenge faced by the firm. 2. **Analyze** the strategic choices made in terms of channels, content, and targeting. 3. **Evaluate** outcomes against defined **KPIs** and **ROI**. 4. **Recommend** alternative strategies, displaying higher-order Bloom's thinking (**Analyze → Evaluate → Create**). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Universal Case Study Dissection Framework (UCDF) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every KTU Digital Marketing case study answer — whether for 3 marks, 7 marks, or 14 marks — must follow this **6-Stage Analytical Scaffold**. Examiners actively scan for these stages in the answer script. ### Stage 1: **Business Context (The "As-Is" Snapshot)** - Industry, market size, target audience. - Pre-campaign digital maturity and pain points. ### Stage 2: **Strategic Objectives (SMART Goals)** - Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. - Examples: "Increase online sales by **30%** in Q3" or "Acquire **100K app installs** in 6 months". ### Stage 3: **Digital Marketing Mix (The Tactical Arsenal)** - Channel selection: SEO + SEM + Social + Email + Influencer. - Content strategy and creative positioning. - Budget allocation across channels. ### Stage 4: **Execution & Campaign Mechanics** - A/B testing, ad copy, landing page optimization. - Personalization tokens, retargeting pixels, lookalike audiences. ### Stage 5: **Performance Measurement (The KPI Dashboard)** - Reach, Impressions, CTR, Engagement, Conversion, CAC, LTV. ### Stage 6: **Outcomes, Learnings & Strategic Recommendations** - What worked? What failed? How can it be improved? ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory for Calculations) > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners often insert a **"Calculate the ROI"** or **"Determine the Conversion Rate"** sub-question inside a 14-mark case study. Memorize these equations verbatim. ### Core Marketing Equations $$ \text{Conversion Rate (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Number of Conversions}}{\text{Total Visitors}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{CTR (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Number of Clicks}}{\text{Number of Impressions}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{CAC} = \frac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{Number of New Customers Acquired}} $$ $$ \text{ROAS} = \frac{\text{Revenue Generated from Ads}}{\text{Total Ad Spend}} $$ $$ \text{ROI (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Net Profit from Campaign}}{\text{Total Campaign Cost}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{LTV} = \text{Average Purchase Value} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan} $$ $$ \text{Engagement Rate (\%)} = \left( \frac{\text{Total Engagements (Likes + Comments + Shares)}}{\text{Total Reach}} \right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Cost per Mille (CPM)} = \left( \frac{\text{Ad Spend}}{\text{Impressions}} \right) \times 1000 $$ $$ \text{Cost per Click (CPC)} = \frac{\text{Ad Spend}}{\text{Number of Clicks}} $$ ### Cheat-Sheet Table: KTU 2024 Formula Reference Card | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Typical Unit | KTU Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Conversion Rate | $\frac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Visitors}} \times 100$ | Percentage | E-commerce case analysis | | CTR | $\frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Ad campaign effectiveness | | CAC | $\frac{\text{Spend}}{\text{Customers}}$ | Currency (₹) | Customer acquisition analysis | | ROAS | $\frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Spend}}$ | Ratio (e.g., 4:1) | Ad profitability check | | ROI | $\frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Overall campaign ROI | | LTV | AOV $\times$ Frequency $\times$ Lifespan | Currency (₹) | Long-term customer value | | Bounce Rate | $\frac{\text{Single Page Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Landing page quality | ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business For KTU B.Tech students — especially those in **CS, IT, AI&DS, and ECE** branches opting for OEPET833 — the case study methodology is industry-critical because: 1. **Product Managers** at firms like Flipkart, Zomato, and Razorpay justify strategy using these exact KPI frameworks. 2. **Growth Hackers** in startups run A/B tests quantified by **Conversion Rate** and **CAC**. 3. **SEO Analysts** pitch clients using **CTR**, **Bounce Rate**, and **Organic Reach** data. 4. **Performance Marketers** in agencies are evaluated on **ROAS** and **LTV:CAC ratios**. > [!NOTE] > The same formulas power **Google Analytics 4 (GA4)**, **Meta Ads Manager**, and **HubSpot dashboards** — tools that any KTU graduate will encounter within their first job. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Five Mandatory Indian + Global Case Studies (KTU Board Favourites) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme examiners expect students to demonstrate analytical depth on **at least 3–5 of the following canonical case studies**. The following five are the most exam-rewarding. --- ### **CASE STUDY 1: DOMINO'S PIZZA – "AnyWare" Digital Ordering Transformation** #### Background - **Industry:** Quick Service Restaurant (QSR). - **Challenge:** Declining in-store traffic; customers preferred ordering via phone, which was error-prone. - **Objective:** Build a **frictionless omnichannel ordering ecosystem** accessible from any device. #### Strategic Digital Mix - **Mobile App** with one-click reordering and pizza tracker. - **"AnyWare" ordering** via Smart TV, smartwatch, Twitter DM, Slack, and voice assistants. - **Gamified loyalty program** with points for every online order. - **Personalized push notifications** based on order history. #### Numerical Performance Snapshot (Exam-Relevant Data) $$ \text{Online Sales Contribution} = 75\% \text{ of total UK sales (post-digital transformation)} $$ $$ \text{Mobile App Downloads} = 10 \text{ Million+ globally} $$ #### Analytical Breakdown (Stage-by-Stage for KTU Answers) 1. **Funnel Optimization:** Domino's collapsed the ordering funnel from **6 steps (phone) → 2 steps (app)**. 2. **Channel Integration:** Unified data layer across web, app, voice, and social channels. 3. **Customer Retention:** Loyalty program increased **repeat purchase frequency by 28%**. 4. **Data-Driven Personalization:** Past orders triggered recommendations ("You ordered a Margherita last Friday — reorder?"). #### Key Takeaway for KTU Answer > Domino's is the textbook example of **Omnichannel Digital Marketing** with measurable KPI uplift. --- ### **CASE STUDY 2: AIRBNB – SEO-Led Growth Hacking** #### Background - **Industry:** Peer-to-peer hospitality / Travel-tech. - **Challenge:** Competing with giants (Hotels.com, Booking.com) with **zero marketing budget** at launch. - **Objective:** Achieve organic, scalable customer acquisition. #### Strategic Digital Mix - **Craigslist Integration Hack:** Airbnb allowed hosts to cross-post listings to Craigslist, tapping into an existing audience. - **Long-Tail SEO Strategy:** Optimized for "unique vacation rentals in [city]" — not generic "hotels in Paris". - **Professional Photography Program:** Free professional photographers for hosts, dramatically increasing listing quality and **CTR**. #### Numerical Performance Snapshot $$ \text{Listing Conversion Rate Uplift} = 2.5\times \text{ after professional photography} $$ $$ \text{Organic Search Traffic Growth} = \text{Multiple-fold via long-tail SEO} $$ #### Analytical Breakdown 1. **Growth Hacking > Paid Ads:** Built **referral loops** and **SEO moats** instead of burning cash on Google Ads. 2. **Supply-Side Quality = Demand-Side Conversion:** Better photos → higher trust → higher bookings. 3. **Network Effects:** Each host listing brought in two new guests, who themselves became potential hosts. #### Key Takeaway > Airbnb is the canonical **SEO + Growth Hacking** case study — memorize the **Craigslist cross-posting hack** for bonus marks. --- ### **CASE STUDY 3: NIKE – "Just Do It" 30th Anniversary + Digital Ecosystem** #### Background - **Industry:** Sportswear / Athleisure. - **Challenge:** Launch the 30th anniversary campaign featuring **Colin Kaepernick** (controversial figure) into a polarized digital audience. - **Objective:** Reinforce brand purpose and drive engagement despite social media backlash risk. #### Strategic Digital Mix - **Hero Video Ad** featuring Kaepernick with the tagline "Believe in something. Even if it means sacrificing everything." - **Social Media Blitz:** #JustDoIt hashtag activated across Twitter, Instagram, YouTube. - **Nike Run Club / Nike Training Club Apps** as owned media assets. #### Numerical Performance Snapshot $$ \text{Online Sales Increase} = 31\% \text{ in the days following campaign launch} $$ $$ \text{Social Media Engagement Spike} = 1.7\times \text{ average benchmark} $$ $$ \text{Brand Sentiment} = + \text{among Gen Z and millennials, despite some backlash} $$ #### Analytical Breakdown 1. **Purpose-Driven Marketing:** Aligned brand with a social cause, creating emotional resonance. 2. **Risk-Taking + Digital Amplification:** The controversial angle generated **earned media** worth millions. 3. **Owned Media Leverage:** Used mobile apps to convert brand engagement into purchase behavior. #### Key Takeaway > Nike is the **Purpose-Driven + Earned Media** case study. Use it for questions on **brand storytelling and social media virality**. --- ### **CASE STUDY 4: ZOMATO / SWIGGY – Hyperlocal Digital Marketing in India** #### Background - **Industry:** Food-tech / Hyperlocal delivery. - **Challenge:** Compete in a price-sensitive Indian market with low margins. - **Objective:** Build user habit and frequency of orders. #### Strategic Digital Mix - **Heavy Performance Marketing Spend** on Google and Meta Ads. - **Influencer Collaborations** with food bloggers. - **Email + Push Notification Marketing** with personalized offers. - **Referral Programs** ("Refer a friend, get ₹100"). - **SEO-Optimized Restaurant Pages** to capture organic discovery intent. #### Numerical Performance Snapshot $$ \text{Average Order Frequency Increase} = 3.5 \text{ orders/month/active user} $$ $$ \text{CAC (Blended)} = ₹150-200 \text{ (illustrative, varies by city)} $$ #### Analytical Breakdown 1. **Multi-Channel Funnel:** Top-of-funnel ads → middle-funnel influencer reviews → bottom-funnel discounts and loyalty coins. 2. **Data-Driven Personalization:** "Craving biryani? Order from Paradise!" — location + past-order + weather signals. 3. **Retention > Acquisition:** The focus shifted from CAC reduction to **LTV maximization** via loyalty programs (**Zomato Gold / Swiggy One**). #### Key Takeaway > Zomato/Swiggy is the **Performance Marketing + Retention** case study. Use it for questions on **CAC vs LTV trade-offs**. --- ### **CASE STUDY 5: CADBURY – Personalized Video Campaign (Shah Rukh Khan AI Avatar)** #### Background - **Industry:** FMCG / Confectionery. - **Challenge:** Personalize celebrity-led advertising for thousands of local retailers across India without high production costs. - **Objective:** Drive local store footfall and online sales of Cadbury products. #### Strategic Digital Mix - **AI + Data Personalization:** Created a **digital avatar of Shah Rukh Khan** that addressed viewers by name and their local store name. - **Programmatic Video Ads** served across YouTube, Facebook, and Instagram. - **Local Store Tie-ins:** Each ad promoted a *specific local store* selling Cadbury. #### Numerical Performance Snapshot $$ \text{CTR Increase} = \text{Multiplier over generic celebrity ads} $$ $$ \text{View-Through Rate} = \text{Significantly above industry benchmark (2-5\%)} $$ $$ \text{Brand Recall} = \text{Among the highest in Cadbury's campaign history} $$ #### Analytical Breakdown 1. **Hyper-Personalization at Scale:** AI + automation made **1,00,000+ personalized variants** possible. 2. **Local-Store-Connect:** Drove **offline conversions** through a digital campaign — bridging **online-to-offline (O2O)**. 3. **Celebrity + Tech Synergy:** Used a beloved brand ambassador in a tech-forward, scalable way. #### Key Takeaway > Cadbury is the **AI-Driven Hyper-Personalization** case study. Use it for questions on **content personalization, programmatic ads, and O2O commerce**. ## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU High-Yield Revision Table) | Case Study | Primary Channel | Core Strategy | Key KPI | KTU Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Domino's** | Omnichannel App | Frictionless ordering | Online sales share | Omnichannel | | **Airbnb** | SEO + Growth Hack | Long-tail organic | Listing conversions | SEO & Growth | | **Nike** | Social + Owned Media | Purpose-driven | Engagement rate | Brand storytelling | | **Zomato/Swiggy** | Performance Ads | Retention & LTV | CAC : LTV ratio | Performance marketing | | **Cadbury** | Programmatic AI | Personalization | CTR uplift | Hyper-personalization | ## 3.3 The Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (For Calculation-Based Questions) > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners love inserting a "Calculate the X" sub-question inside a 14-mark case study. Practice the following. ### **Worked Example: Zomato's Bangalore Campaign** **Given Data:** - Total ad spend on a Bangalore campaign = **₹5,00,000** - Impressions generated = **2,00,000** - Clicks on the ad = **8,000** - Orders placed = **400** - Average order value = **₹350** - Repeat orders from acquired customers in 6 months = **600 orders** @ ₹350 **Step 1: Calculate CTR** $$ \text{CTR} = \left( \frac{8000}{200000} \right) \times 100 = 4\% $$ **Step 2: Calculate Conversion Rate (Click → Order)** $$ \text{Conversion Rate} = \left( \frac{400}{8000} \right) \times 100 = 5\% $$ **Step 3: Calculate CAC** $$ \text{CAC} = \frac{500000}{400} = \text{₹}1250 \text{ per customer} $$ **Step 4: Calculate Revenue (First Purchase + Repeat)** $$ \text{Total Revenue} = (400 + 600) \times 350 = \text{₹}3,50,000 $$ **Step 5: Calculate ROAS** $$ \text{ROAS} = \frac{350000}{500000} = 0.7 $$ > [!WARNING] > An ROAS of 0.7 indicates a **loss-making campaign in the short term** — but if the repeat customers yield an LTV of ₹2,000+, the campaign is profitable in the **long run**. KTU students must comment on this **short-term vs. long-term ROI distinction** to score full marks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Universal Digital Marketing Funnel (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Awareness Stage - It measures the revenue generated for every unit of currency spent on advertising. $$ \text{ROAS} = \frac{\text{Revenue Generated from Ads}}{\text{Total Ad Spend}} $$ - **Benchmark (1 mark):** A **healthy ROAS is 4:1** — meaning ₹4 revenue is generated for every ₹1 spent on ads. - **Use Case (0.5 mark):** ROAS is the primary metric for performance marketing campaigns on Google Ads and Meta Ads. --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **"What is the significance of the LTV:CAC ratio in digital marketing? State the golden ratio."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **LTV (Lifetime Value) (1 mark):** The total revenue a business can expect from a single customer account throughout the business relationship. - **CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost) (1 mark):** The cost of acquiring a new customer, including ad spend, sales effort, and onboarding. - **Golden Ratio (1 mark):** The ideal **LTV:CAC ratio is 3:1** — indicating sustainable growth. A ratio below 1:1 means the business is losing money on every customer. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) for 14-mark questions offer an **internal choice**. You must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Both are provided below with full model solutions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): "Domino's Pizza Digital Transformation Case"** **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** **"Analyze the digital marketing strategy adopted by Domino's Pizza to become a technology-first QSR brand. Discuss the role of the mobile app and the 'AnyWare' ordering initiative."** **Model Answer:** 1. **Background (1.5 marks):** Domino's, a global QSR chain, faced declining in-store sales and operational inefficiencies in phone-based ordering. The brand repositioned itself as a **"technology company that sells pizza"** — a digitally-driven enterprise. 2. **Mobile App Strategy (2.5 marks):** - One-tap reordering using saved preferences. - Real-time **pizza tracker** (Order Placed → In Oven → Out for Delivery → Delivered). - In-app coupons, loyalty points, and voice ordering. - Result: **Over 75% of UK sales** came through digital channels post-transformation. 3. **AnyWare Initiative (2 marks):** - Ordering from **any device** — Smart TV, smartwatch, Twitter DM, Slack, voice assistants (Amazon Echo). - Frictionless ordering across **20+ digital touchpoints**. - Reinforced the **omnichannel strategy** — meeting customers *where they are*. 4. **Outcomes (1 mark):** - Increased **average order frequency**. - Reduced **order errors** by 30–40%. - Strengthened **customer lifetime value (LTV)** via the loyalty ecosystem. --- **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** **"Evaluate the KPIs Domino's should monitor to measure the success of its digital transformation. Use formulas where applicable."** **Model Answer:** 1. **Channel-Level KPIs (2 marks):** - **App Downloads and Active Users (DAU/MAU ratio)**. - **Push Notification Opt-in Rate**. 2. **Engagement KPIs (2 marks):** - **CTR** of in-app promotions: $\text{CTR} = \frac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$. - **Engagement Rate** on social media campaigns. - **Bounce Rate** on the website. 3. **Conversion and Revenue KPIs (2 marks):** - **Conversion Rate** from app session → order placed. - **ROAS** for paid social ads: $\text{ROAS} = \frac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{Ad Spend}}$. - **Average Order Value (AOV)** and **Frequency of Reordering**. 4. **Retention KPIs (1 mark):** - **LTV** of loyalty program members vs. non-members. - **Churn Rate** of app users. > [!TIP] > A complete 7-mark answer must include **at least 4 KPIs**, with **at least 2 explicit formulas**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for each missing formula in calculation-heavy sub-questions. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): "Cadbury's AI-Powered Shah Rukh Khan Campaign"** **Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** **"Explain Cadbury's 'Not Just a Cadbury Ad' campaign. How did AI and personalization transform a traditional celebrity endorsement into a hyper-personalized digital experience?"** **Model Answer:** 1. **Campaign Context (1.5 marks):** - Launched around Diwali 2021 by **Ogilvy** for Cadbury (Mondelez India). - Objective: Help **local retailers** recover post-pandemic by personalizing a celebrity campaign for thousands of small shops. 2. **AI + Celebrity Avatar (2.5 marks):** - Used AI to create a **digital avatar of Shah Rukh Khan** that addressed each viewer by **name** and mentioned their **local store**. - Generated **1,00,000+ personalized ad variants** algorithmically. - The celebrity became a *scalable* brand ambassador — impossible in traditional advertising. 3. **Channel Deployment (1.5 marks):** - Served via **programmatic video ads** on YouTube, Facebook, and Instagram. - Each ad dynamically inserted the **viewer's name and local store**. 4. **Strategic Impact (1.5 marks):** - **Hyper-personalization at scale** — a textbook case of using AI in marketing. - **Online-to-Offline (O2O) Bridge** — digital ads drove physical store footfall. - Significant **brand recall and CTR uplift** versus generic celebrity ads. --- **Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** **"Calculate the campaign's ROAS given the following hypothetical data, and recommend two improvements to scale it."** **Given Data:** - Total Campaign Cost = ₹**2 Crore** (AI tech + media spend) - Total Conversions (clicks leading to local store visit) = **5,00,000** - Average Order Value per Store Visit = **₹600** - Repeat Purchases (Next 3 months) = **10,00,000** orders @ ₹600 **Model Answer:** 1. **Total Revenue (2 marks):** - First-purchase revenue = $5{,}00{,}000 \times 600 = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}00{,}000$ - Repeat revenue = $10{,}00{,}000 \times 600 = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}00{,}000$ - **Total Revenue = ₹9,00,00,000** 2. **ROAS Calculation (1.5 marks):** - $\text{ROAS} = \frac{9{,}00{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 4.5$ 3. **ROI Calculation (1.5 marks):** - Net Profit = ₹9,00,00,000 − ₹2,00,00,000 = ₹7,00,00,000 - $\text{ROI} = \left( \frac{7{,}00{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 350\%$ 4. **Strategic Recommendations (2 marks):** - **Recommendation 1:** Expand the AI avatar model to other regional celebrities in **Tamil, Telugu, Bengali, and Marathi** markets for linguistic personalization. - **Recommendation 2:** Integrate with **WhatsApp Business API** to send personalized follow-up coupons directly to customers, boosting **LTV**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT SKIP** > > 1. **Skipping the LTV Discussion (–2 marks):** KTU examiners specifically check whether students discuss **long-term value**, not just first-purchase conversion. Always calculate repeat-purchase revenue. > 2. **Forgetting Units (–1 mark):** Mention **₹ (rupees)**, **percentages (%)**, and **ratios** explicitly. A bare "4.5" without context is incomplete. > 3. **No Recommendations (–2 marks):** A 14-mark case study question without strategic recommendations is treated as incomplete. Always end with **"Recommendation 1... Recommendation 2..."**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This in Your Study Room** - [ ] **5 Canonical Case Studies:** Domino's (Omnichannel), Airbnb (SEO Growth), Nike (Purpose-Driven), Zomato (Performance Marketing), Cadbury (AI Personalization). Memorize **at least 3** in depth. - [ ] **The 6-Stage UCDF Framework:** Context → Objectives → Strategy → Execution → Measurement → Recommendations. Use this scaffold for **every** case study answer. - [ ] **Golden Ratios to Memorize:** - **ROAS = 4:1** (Healthy) - **LTV:CAC = 3:1** (Sustainable) - **CTR Industry Average = 2–5%** - **Engagement Rate (Organic) = 1–3%** - [ ] **Formulas to Quote Verbatim:** Conversion Rate, CTR, CAC, ROAS, ROI, LTV, Bounce Rate. Use **LaTeX formatting** in answer scripts when permitted. - [ ] **The Funnel Vocabulary:** TOFU (Awareness), MOFU (Consideration), BOFU (Conversion), Retention, Advocacy. Use these terms liberally — examiners love them. - [ ] **Bloom's Cognitive Level Mapping:** A KTU 14-mark case study tests **Analyze + Evaluate + Create**. Your answer must include **analysis, evaluation, AND recommendations** to score above **12/14**. - [ ] **Channel-Objective Mapping:** Awareness → Social + Display, Lead Gen → SEO + Email, Conversion → Search + Retargeting, Retention → CRM + Loyalty. - [ ] **Indian Context Edge:** Zomato, Swiggy, Cadbury, Amul, Flipkart, and Cred are **favoured KTU Indian case studies**. Memorize at least **one Indian brand** per answer to score **context marks**. - [ ] **The Three "Always Add" Sentences:** 1. *"This campaign demonstrates the strategic use of [SEO/Omnichannel/AI/Personalization]..."* 2. *"Measured by [CTR/ROAS/CAC], the campaign achieved a [benchmark/comparison]..."* 3. *"Future iterations could benefit from [LTV optimization/multi-channel integration]..."* - [ ] **The Pitfall to Avoid:** Never present a case study as a *narrative*. Frame it as a **structured analytical argument** with clear headings, formulas, and recommendations. - [ ] **Last-Minute Power Move:** If you forget formulas in the exam hall, write the **logical English form** first ("Revenue divided by Ad Spend") followed by the **symbolic form** — partial credit is awarded.
